Modern Love label output emphasizing warehouse spirit and contemporary deep techno aesthetics with Detroit and dub influences. Mix combines exclusive and unreleased material from two of the label's key producers, creating a cohesive 50-minute journey suited for immersive listening.

Mix reflects Kalabrese's drumming roots and live orchestral sensibilities within a minimal house/techno framework, drawing from his parents' record collection (Hendrix, Coltrane, Zappa). Tracklist includes Detroit house pioneers (Kevin Saunderson, Mike Dunn), contemporary minimal techno (Efdemin, Prosumer), and world music samples (Chilean folk, Latin rhythms), creating an eclectic yet cohesive narrative. The remix of Thomas Dolby by Villalobos and Henrik Schwarz's take on Camille exemplify the 'foolish, eccentric and soulful' aesthetic described.

Mix showcases Sasse's Moodmusic label aesthetic with trance-inflected house and emotional Euro-techno. Tracks from Dirt Crew, Defected, and Aus Music indicate a contemporary deep/tech house sensibility rooted in Berlin club culture. Editorial describes progression from energetic to introspective, supporting warm-up to early peak-time trajectory.

Pole curates a carefully sequenced mix bridging classic dub reggae (Roy Lee, Johnny Osbourne, Althea and Donna) with contemporary UK dubstep and post-dubstep (Shackleton, Mala, Benga, Untold). His signature Waldorf 4-Pole filtered sound frames the mix through unreleased overdubs and interludes, creating a cohesive narrative about dub's evolutionary continuum. The emphasis is experimental and introspective rather than peak-time club energy.

Mix showcases Derek Plaslaiko's Detroit roots with a curated selection spanning stripped-down minimal techno to more melodic remixes. Strong presence of key labels (Drumcode, Poker Flat, Playhouse) and artists associated with the minimal techno underground. Editorial context emphasizes the Bunker residency influence and touring momentum under the 'Death Is Nothing to Fear' banner.

Mix recorded live at Robert Johnson club in Offenbach. Features key artists from the contemporary Frankfurt/Mannheim deep house scene (Villalobos, Dimbiman, Ben Klock via Klockworks). Editorial context emphasizes the intersection of modern deepness with old school aesthetics, positioning this as part of a new deep movement bridging techno and house. Tracklist includes unreleased material and edits, suggesting a curated DJ selection rather than commercial releases.

Mix showcases Anders Ilar's signature sound that blends ambient pioneers (Tangerine Dream, Jean Michel Jarre) with contemporary melodic techno. Second half features six of Ilar's own tracks. Strong Cologne school influence despite never releasing on Kompakt. Featured artists (DeepChord, Rod Modell, Ezekiel Honig, Ripperton) reinforce the dub techno and ambient techno aesthetic.

Kevin Saunderson as Belleville Three founder brings authoritative Detroit techno lineage mixed with accessible house sensibilities from his Inner City era. Mix balances underground credibility with broad appeal.

Dinky's set exemplifies deep minimal techno with a focus on stripped-back, atmospheric production. The tracklist features key figures in minimal techno (Ricardo Villalobos, Radio Slave/Panorama Garage, Tobi Neumann) alongside her own Horizontal label artists. The set's trajectory is introspective rather than peak-time, building subtle tension through restrained sound design. The DJ Assault inclusion adds a slightly unexpected footwork/Detroit flavor but doesn't derail the overall minimal aesthetic.

Mix emphasizes deep, dark house with strong Detroit techno influences. Editorial context emphasizes the 'deep vibes' philosophy and floating hypnotic quality rather than peak-time energy. Strong presence of minimalist production and soulful Detroit/US house heritage through artists like Moodymann and Blaze.

Todd Terje's specialty is creative edits of diverse source material spanning soul, reggae, R&B, and classic rock. The mix builds narratively over 31 minutes, designed as a complete DJ set despite time constraints. Strong emphasis on unconventional disco interpretations rather than pure house/techno, following the Idjut Boys/Lindstrom lineage.

Mix heavily influenced by UK bass music, grime, and American urban styles (Baltimore club, Miami bass). The Count & Sinden blend disparate genres into cohesive dancefloor energy—funk, dancehall, electro, and hip-hop collide in a rambunctious, non-purist approach. Studio-recorded with Ableton, emphasizing straightforward dancefloor impact over technical excess.

Richie Hawtin's celebratory 100th RA podcast showcasing his transition to fully digital DJing after abandoning vinyl. Mix emphasizes the sonic possibilities opened by Traktor/Ableton integration, blending stripped-back minimal techno with deeper house elements. Features artists associated with Minus and contemporary minimal labels like Poker Flat, Desolat, and Raster-Noton. The 60-minute set functions as a creative laboratory exploring the dissolving boundary between studio production and live performance.

Mix emphasizes lush, analog-driven minimal techno with a focus on newer productions alongside classic tracks. Brocksieper's approach balances newer releases with timeless pieces, showcasing the refined Cologne techno tradition.

Mix reflects Connaisseur Supérieur's distinctive aesthetic: melodic tech house with emotional depth. Blends contemporary productions with classic house and minimal techno. Curated by label head Alex Flitsch to showcase 'warm feeling, timelessness, and emotionality' rather than pure label output.

Mix blends obscure exotica, dub reggae, downtempo, and ambient music with an emphasis on mood over beats. Editorial context emphasizes vibes, Balearic sensibility, and eclectic sourcing from 50s lounge, soul, film soundtracks, and reggae.

Donato Dozzy's signature 'microtrance' style blends minimal techno foundations with dub textures and ambient drift, creating a distinctly hypnotic and introspective sound designed for extended late-night listening

Nôze defies conventional genre classification—their 'boozecore' style combines electronic elements with vaudeville theatricality, live performance antics, and absurdist vocals. This live set from Mutek emphasizes the chaotic, comedic performance aspect over traditional dance music structure.

Laurent Garnier's signature style blending house and techno with jazz instrumentation, soul samples (Nina Simone bootleg), and progressive touches. The mix emphasizes groove-oriented, soulful dance music rather than minimal or industrial variants.

Traum Recordings retrospective showcasing the label's signature blend of minimalist and melodic techno with ambient elements and Detroit/early English techno references. Key artists (Dominik Eulberg, Nathan Fake, Minilogue, Gabriel Ananda) define an introspective, harmonically rich aesthetic that bridges ambient and dancefloor music.

A carefully curated mix of classic soul, funk, disco and R&B records spanning multiple decades, thematically centered around emotional vulnerability and relationships. The editorial context emphasizes The Mole's expertise in finding obscure, soulful records that reward deep listening and crate-digging. While The Mole is known for organic house music production, this podcast is entirely devoted to vintage soul and funk standards rather than contemporary electronic music.

The mix epitomizes the early-to-mid 2000s UK sound that bridges dubstep and deep/dub techno. Key producers like Shackleton, Martyn, Peverelist, and Shed represent the distinctly British approach of applying dubstep's spacious, textural production to house and techno frameworks. The editorial framing emphasizes this liminal space—'housestep' or 'dubhouse'—where brittle, metallic sonics meet hypnotic grooves. Tracks heavily feature remixes and unreleased material, reflecting the experimental, iterative nature of the scene.

Stimming's signature sound centers on gently melodic, emotionally resonant house music with organic elements. The mix showcases his skill composing cohesive original productions and remixes, with notable use of folk samples (Violetta Parra). Recorded live in-studio using Ableton Live, creating a club-ready yet introspective atmosphere that avoids repetitiveness despite featuring primarily his own work.

Mark E's signature style centers on extended grooves and hypnotic repetition rather than dynamic builds. The mix emphasizes soul samples (Marvin Gaye, Grace Jones, Amp Fiddler) layered over deliberate, throbbing beats in a disco-influenced but contemporary context. Re-edits are a core aesthetic.

Mix emphasizes classic Detroit techno (Jeff Mills, The Persuader) alongside experimental and minimal tracks. Editorial context highlights live musicianship and jam-band ethos, though this particular mix uses DJ curation rather than live performance. Strong emphasis on funk-informed techno and conceptual/artistic electronic music.

The mix showcases Aeroplane's signature sound of modern disco and balearic house with an emphasis on letting tracks breathe. Strong focus on disco-inflected house music and remixes from their own production work.

Mix emphasizes melodic minimalism with sweeping chords and rhythmic subtlety, drawing from the Berlin/Amsterdam underground scene. Cat-themed BBC samples add playful, unconventional texture throughout.

Mix reflects Gerd Janson's philosophy of house music with Scandinavian and German minimalist influences. Features key artists from Running Back and Permanent Vacation labels. The tracklist emphasizes deep, cosmic house with dub and ambient textures—'Computer Incarnations for World Peace' aesthetic is evident throughout.

Alan Abrahams' Portable/Bodycode set showcases a sophisticated blend of house and techno with experimental elements—flutes, theremins, and other organic textures counterbalance electronic foundations. The narrative emphasizes accessibility alongside idiosyncrasy, with deep ties to his African heritage and UK dance culture. Released on respected imprints (Perlon, Spectral Sound, Yore, Sud Electronic), the mix functions as a meditative, journey-oriented listening experience rather than peak-time dancefloor material.

Mix celebrates Italo disco and disco sounds with genuine emotion; features classic Detroit and Chicago house cuts alongside contemporary productions; extensive use of film score samples (Argento, Carpenter) creates dark, atmospheric framing; curator philosophy emphasizes sincerity over irony in engagement with 'cheesy' synthetic aesthetics.

Greg Wilson's signature edit-based approach dominates this mix, splicing soul, funk, and house records with electronic elements. The tracklist emphasizes classic soul and funk source material (Gary Davis, Rufus & Chaka Khan, Eddie Drennon) remixed and mashed with contemporary dance tracks. The downtempo tag reflects the blurb's description, though the mix oscillates between soulful slow-burn grooves and accessible house elements.

Steffi's signature sound centers on deep house with techno and acid elements, curating across eras (Pépé Bradock's classic deep house, Omar-S's Detroit minimalism, contemporary producers like STL). The mix balances introspection with dancefloor physicality, reflecting her Panorama Bar residency style. Recorded at home, emphasizing personal taste over high-energy production.

Lee Jones deliberately constructs a pensive, film-score-influenced mix that opens and closes with orchestral material (Lalo Schifrin's Cool Hand Luke theme) before guiding through sparse, meditative electronic production. The bulk features Get Physical Music, Hessle Audio, and Stil Vor Talent releases—hallmarks of the contemporary minimal/microhouse aesthetic. Recorded at home in Ableton Live.

Mix showcases Argentine minimal techno and deep house scene. Simbad blends early dub/reggae influences with contemporary minimal and deep house. Features local Argentine artists (May Segui, Cristobal Paz) alongside international names (Perc, Dennis Ferrer). The set demonstrates the hypnotic, stripped-back aesthetic of minimal techno with soulful deep house elements rather than peak-time techno.

Nearly all tracks from Underground Resistance, the legendary Detroit techno collective. The mix celebrates UR's pioneering blend of hard techno, industrial elements, and hi-tech jazz influences. Track titles evoke sci-fi and political themes characteristic of UR's artistic vision. The Black Dog's editorial context emphasizes UR's innovation and continued relevance in pushing electronic music forward.

Derrick Carter's mix heavily features his own remixes and unreleased edits (marked BHQ for Boompty HeadQuarters). The tracklist demonstrates classic Chicago house sensibilities with soulful vocal samples, jazzy elements (St. James Infirmary Blues), and an emphasis on groove-oriented, bouncy house music rather than harder techno variants. The editorial context emphasizes Carter's legendary status in 90s Chicago house resurgence.

Move D's set showcases his signature warm, deep approach to house and ambient techno. The mix balances released material (Running Back, Compost, Uzuri) with numerous unreleased tracks and collaborations, reflecting his prescient move away from dry minimalism toward organic, emotionally resonant electronic music. The Tape Club Berlin setting and curated selection capture Moufang in a reflective, experimental moment.

Mix showcases Motorcitysoul's signature melodic house sound with strong emphasis on deep, driving tech house. Tracklist features established deep house figures (Kerri Chandler, Steve Lawler, Sebo K) alongside their own productions, reflecting their position in the contemporary deep house scene rather than trend-chasing. The selection maintains consistent energy suitable for club play while emphasizing soulful, lush arrangements.

Jus-Ed's mix showcases his signature raw house sound with deep, minimal techno elements. The presence of Dial (Lawrence), Underground Quality stalwarts (AntonZap), and other underground producers emphasizes his role as a tastemaker. The tracklist leans toward stripped-down, groove-oriented house and minimal techno rather than peak-time club music, reflecting a home-listening context and introspective mood despite the dance floor potential.

Mix exclusively features Meanwhile label material. Editorial emphasizes sophisticated dub techno sound that avoids derivative Basic Channel aesthetics. Vinyl-to-DAT recording with effects and field recordings creates immersive 74-minute journey with indistinct vocal samples and atmospheric space.

Mix emphasizes melancholic melodies over minimal beats, blending accessibility with sophistication. Lee Burridge's signature style combines modern dancefloor sensibilities with introspective, atmospheric elements suited for deep listening.

Mix reflects Todd's evolution from deep house roots toward harder-edged tech house. Features key players like Jamie Jones, Steve Lawler, and Catz N' Dogz. Heavy on remixes and unreleased material. Dirtybird connection signals modern tech house sensibility with minimal techno undercurrents. Strong emphasis on driving beats and sophisticated production.
Mix focuses on yacht rock and Balearic-influenced selections with 80s pop elements, curated for the Erection Section's slower, more romantic dancefloor aesthetic rather than peak-time clubbing.

Mix showcases Motor City Drum Ensemble's signature approach of splicing disco and soul loops into house music, bridging Detroit and Chicago traditions. Editorial context emphasizes the retrospective-yet-forward-thinking aesthetic and deep connection to Detroit despite being produced in Stuttgart.

Mix emphasizes the soulful, vintage-leaning deep house aesthetic that Trus'me is known for. Tracks from Prime Numbers label and associated artists showcase a warm, organic approach to house music with Detroit roots and contemporary production sensibilities. The editorial context emphasizes the 'soulful spirit of vintage US house music' which is the core identity here.

This mix showcases Howells' love of pop, funk, and soul music layered over his progressive house foundation. The tracklist emphasizes classic funk, soul, and hip-hop records (Isley Brothers, Marvin Gaye, Herbie Hancock, Beastie Boys, DJ Shadow) remixed and edited for dancefloor context, creating an eclectic, groove-oriented DJ set rather than a pure techno or house mix. The editorial context explicitly describes this as 'poppier' and celebrating Howells' passion for pop music beyond his progressive house reputation.

Dettmann's mix showcases his curated 'quest for the optimal track' philosophy with essentialist techno across multiple eras and regions. Heavy emphasis on Ostgut Ton and affiliated labels, Detroit pioneers (Rolando, Common Factor), contemporary minimalists (Levon Vincent, Scuba), and breakbeat experimentalists (Shackleton). Mix demonstrates mastery of extended DJ sets that balance hypnotic grooves with subtle rhythmic complexity.
The Orb's seminal work in ambient house; explicitly described as 'mostly beatless' and designed as a decompression soundtrack for New Year recovery

Mix heavily features Detroit techno influences with epic melodic builds, anchored by key Detroit and contemporary techno artists (Kerri Chandler, Delano Smith, Marco Carola). Labels like Sandwell District, Rekids, and Plus 8 confirm dark, minimalist techno aesthetic. Editorial context emphasizes Sebastien San's journey from robotic funk through Chicago house to deep Detroit techno reverence.

Mix reflects Breitbarth's signature blend of hypnotic techno with classic house swing. Strong presence of minimal and deep house from labels like Oslo Records, Playhouse, and Freerange. Editorial context emphasizes Cologne influences and Berlin club heritage.

Disco Bloodbath represents the contemporary London disco revival with roots in classic disco, Italo, and early house. Their style emphasizes sophisticated selection and mixing skill rather than trend-chasing, creating a signature sound that elevated London's club scene circa 2007-2009.

A curated journey through electronic music history featuring canonical ambient and kosmische works, with Pivot's own contemporary takes blending synth heritage with live instrumentation. The mix emphasizes atmospheric depth and synthesizer exploration over dance or club contexts.

Mix showcases Peacefrog Records catalog with strong Detroit techno lineage (Glenn Underground, DBX, Theo Parrish) blended with house and electro traditions. Passarani's curation reflects his personal history bridging American and European dance music since early 90s acid house boom. Fast-paced 45-minute set maintains consistent club-oriented energy with emphasis on synthesizer-driven, funk-inflected grooves.

Josh Wink draws on his legendary Philly house roots while exploring deeper, more experimental territories. The mix spans from classic acid house (Adonis, Wink's own catalog) through contemporary deep house and minimal techno, with dub and spoken word elements adding sophistication. The editorial context emphasizes his evolution from 90s acid house star to a more introspective, studio-focused artist engaging with modern minimal sounds.

Brinkmann's signature approach: conceptual electronic music transformed into dancefloor material through custom dual-armed turntable manipulation. Mix blends stripped-down minimal techno with unconventional funk and electro elements, grounded in the Max Ernst label aesthetic and Cologne's experimental electronic tradition.

Surgeon's mix reflects his toughened industrial sound and deep connections to both UK underground and Berlin techno scenes. Heavy use of remixes and unreleased material showcases his artist-selection ethos. Features collaborators from Tresor, Rephlex, and other influential labels. Mix balances brutalist minimalism with abstract industrial textures, characteristic of his House of God residency aesthetic.

DJ Koze curates a cerebral, evolving mix that prioritizes depth over dancefloor immediacy. The selection spans minimal and experimental techno with deep house elements, featuring artists like Mount Kimbie, Moodymann, and Ben Klock. The editorial context explicitly describes his preference for music that 'becomes slower and sexier the longer the night gets,' reflected in a carefully considered, non-anthemic approach to DJing.

Mix emphasizes deep, Afro-influenced house music with spiritual, soul-guided selection. Culoe De Song's own productions ('Ambush', 'The Bright Forest') anchor the set. Strong Innervisions and Offering Recordings presence. Recorded live on DJR 400 mixer in Johannesburg.

Mix blends UK dubstep pioneers (Burial, Loefah, Mala) with Berlin minimal techno (Ben Klock, Scuba, Shed), reflecting DJ Pete's dual allegiances to Hard Wax and the underground dubstep scene. The tracklist shows a deliberate synthesis of dub-influenced techno and dubstep, creating a hypnotic, bass-heavy journey that bridges two distinct scenes from the mid-2000s.

Mix showcases Linkwood and House of Traps' broad palette rooted in Detroit house and acid traditions, with strong disco and nu-funk influences. The tracklist features classic house cuts from Trax and Clone alongside contemporary Firecracker productions, emphasizing lush arrangements and acapella layering over functional dancefloor logic.

Losoul/Peter Kremeier is a Frankfurt Playhouse stalwart known for 'coolest m.f. in minimal house' sensibility. Mix progresses from spacious minimal through organic fare, reflecting his signature style of reliable funk-inflected minimal and deep house.

Mix reflects Booka Shade's foundational role in German tech house and their Get Physical label aesthetic—sleek, synth-rich, and designed for sustained club play. Tracklist balances deep, hypnotic cuts with driving dancefloor tracks, anchored by label staples (DJ T., M.A.N.D.Y.) and contemporary producers working in similar minimal-to-tech-house space. Editorial context emphasizes their 90s synth pop roots and subsequent evolution into electronic production, which informs the polished, melodic approach evident here.

Bill Brewster's mix bridges soul, funk, and early disco influences with contemporary house selections. Heavy emphasis on classic funk and soul records (Curtis Mayfield, Merry Clayton, John Martyn) paired with modern house and leftfield electronic tracks. The curation reflects his DJ History expertise—sourcing rare, historically significant records and reimagined edits. This is a thoughtful, knowledge-driven mix that prioritizes musicality and soul over peak-time energy.

Ellen Allien's style centers on abstract and minimal techno with acid house roots from her London days, defining Berlin's electronic music landscape post-1990s. Her approach emphasizes studio experimentation and uncompromising sound design.

A contemplative dub techno journey blending classic Detroit minimalism (Model 500, Maurizio) with contemporary Echospace-affiliated artists. Heavy emphasis on reverb-soaked, atmospheric production and reggae/dub fundamentals. Mix showcases Intrusion's love of reggae culture alongside deep techno roots.

Moonstarr's mix exemplifies early-2000s beat culture, blending hip-hop fundamentals with house, breaks, and jazzy lounge elements. The tracklist shows heavy remixing of his own work alongside breakbeat and house-influenced pieces with jazz and soul samples. This is curated, cosmopolitan listening music rather than peak-time club fare—reflective of his background remixing for electronic pioneers like Carl Craig and Jazzanova.

Louderbach's curated mix blends Troy Pierce's tenebrous minimal techno production with Gibby Miller's gothic sensibilities and EBM/new wave influences. The tracklist spans foundational electronic music (Silver Apples, Spacemen 3, Cluster) through contemporary minimal techno (Plastikman, Losoul) and experimental electronic works (Drexciya, William Basinski), reflecting the duo's synthesis of 80s post-punk/EBM aesthetics with contemporary dark techno production.

The tracklist is remarkably eclectic, mixing classic rock (Talking Heads, Jimi Hendrix), soul/funk (Michael McDonald), indie/alternative (Animal Collective, Chromatics, Cat Power), and leftfield electronic producers (Omar-S, Woolfy). Despite the editorial emphasis on house and techno heritage, this mix prioritizes smooth, summery aesthetics across multiple genres rather than pure dance music. Troxler's signature 'clinically produced' aesthetic surfaces through curated deep house selections alongside unexpected rock and soul covers, creating an sophisticated, album-oriented listening experience rather than a typical dancefloor mix.

Tony Lionni's mix exemplifies contemporary European house-techno fusion, drawing from respected imprints (Clone, Ostgut Ton, R&S) and featuring his own prolific output. The tracklist balances deep, hypnotic grooves with stripped-back minimal techno, reflecting his Manchester roots and Berlin residency. Labels like Jimpster, Slam, and Len Faki confirm the deep house and minimal techno aesthetic. Not purely hard or industrial, but sophisticated and melodically aware.

Levon Vincent's mix showcases a sophisticated blend of deep, minimal house and techno with strong Detroit and Chicago roots. The selection emphasizes hypnotic grooves, soulful vocal touches, and understated production that reflects the emerging New York underground scene centered around Underground Quality and Deconstruct labels. Tracks like 'Chicago Nights' and the presence of Detroit-connected artists (Marcel Dettmann) underscore the Midwest influence. Overall vibe is introspective yet dancefloor-ready, designed for deeper, more thoughtful club listening rather than peak-time energy.

Mix balances deep, melodic house with subtle techno elements. Features releases from landmark labels (Running Back, DFA, Clone, Delsin) known for sophisticated, understated electronic music. Editorial describes 'deep house, techno and a couple of nice surprises'—the Yusef Lateef jazz sample and intricate remixes reflect a thoughtful, album-oriented approach rather than peak-time club energy.

Giles Smith of secretsundaze showcases his deep house philosophy through a curated selection of Detroit and Chicago house pioneers (Omar-S, 2000 and One, Agent X on Planet E) alongside contemporary deep house artists. The tracklist emphasizes soulful, meditative house music with minimal techno influences—aligned with his Faith parties residency roots and complementary to the secretsundaze brand. Tracks like Osunlade's 'Pride' and Rick Wade's 'I Believe' exemplify the warm, spiritual deep house aesthetic he champions.

Lawrence's signature style blends classic Detroit/Chicago deep house with contemporary minimal aesthetics. The mix emphasizes melody and emotional depth over pure dancefloor intensity, featuring key artists like Theo Parrish, Mike Huckaby, Moodymann, and Rick Wade—all central to the deep house and Detroit sound. Vinyl craftsmanship and sonic warmth are core to this selection.

nsi. is the live collaboration project of Tobias Freund and Max Loderbauer, known for their inventive minimal techno and electro output produced on vintage analog equipment including Roland drum machines and Doepfer modular synth. The mix showcases their trippy live aesthetic.

Pisco Sour blends minimal techno with Latin elements and ethnic samples. The duo (Dandy Jack/Martin Schopf and Butane/Andrew Rasse) emphasizes experimentation over club convention, with strong Berlin influence despite their diverse origins.
Mix bridges minimal house/techno with indigenous Cuban music, featuring jazz-funk and disco elements throughout

Mix emphasizes harsh textures, industrial processes, and challenging sound design over dancefloor accessibility. Strong presence of classic industrial/EBM touchstones (Throbbing Gristle, 808 State, Dopplereffekt) alongside contemporary experimental techno. Curated with editorial content emphasizing confrontational aesthetic and DIY punk ethos.

Graeme Clark (The Revenge) curates a disco-leaning house mix featuring funk samples, dubbed-out elements, and reissued soul material. Strong emphasis on edits and reworkings of classic soul/funk records alongside contemporary deep house and dub techno influences. Scottish underground aesthetic with international reach.

labels: Cologne-based aesthetic with South American roots
notes: Matias Aguayo's Cómeme label showcases a distinctive blend of techno and house with strong Latin influences (dembow, cumbia references evident in tracklist). The BumBumBox street party concept and editorial emphasis on 'fun' and 'lampoon[ing] minimal techno trends' indicate a deliberately playful, irreverent approach that eschews the serious minimalism of his earlier work. Heavy use of Latin percussion, vocal samples, and acid-tinged synths throughout.

Mix showcases Peter Van Hoesen's distinctive style—eschewing pure minimalism for funkier, more textural techno. Features key collaborators from Berlin and European experimental techno scenes (Speedy J, Donato Dozzy, Shed, Alva Noto). The tracklist demonstrates a careful arc that builds BPMs and intensity throughout, characteristic of Van Hoesen's DJ approach. Strong presence of Time To Express releases and Berlin techno labels (Ostgut Ton, Delsin, Fachwerk). New Beat heritage of Brussels influences the grooved, percussive approach.

Frankie Knuckles' signature style blends soulful disco traditions with early house music. The tracklist emphasizes deep, melodic house with strong soul and R&B vocal samples, reflecting his Paradise Garage and Warehouse residency influences.

FaltyDL's mix blends the 2-step/UK garage template he pioneered with exploratory IDM, dubstep, and drum & bass. Heavy Planet Mu presence reflects the label's experimental electronic aesthetic. Mix showcases both ambient passages (Floating Points) and kinetic breaks, establishing FaltyDL's eclectic approach to beat-driven music.

Erol Alkan's Disco 3000 project emphasizes disco as the primary flavor, blended with house and electronic elements. The mix reflects his known approach of mashups, remixes, and genre-blending, with particular emphasis on disco culture and dancefloor appeal.

Mix emphasizes disco-inflected house with strong groovy, sample-based character. Key artists like Gui Boratto, Ben Klock, and Junkie XL suggest melodic techno and house influences. The editorial context heavily emphasizes disco credentials and the 'current disco revival' movement, so Disco is essential even though not every track may be purely disco-classified.

Mix showcases Modeselektor's signature genre-fluid approach, blending Detroit techno (Robert Hood, Maurizio), UK dubstep/garage (Untold, Joy Orbison, Headhunter), and contemporary club music. Key tracks from Hotflush, Hessle Audio, and Tempa establish the UK underground DNA. Second Phase's 'Mentasm' anchors the acid/rave heritage. The inclusion of Buraka Som Sistema and L-Vis 1990 adds global club culture dimension. Predominantly instrumental with emphasis on production craft and dancefloor functionality rather than accessibility.

Landstrumm is known for stylistic range across techno, bass music, and dubstep. This RA podcast features his live set, likely showcasing his trademark thick low-end with contemporary bass music and instrumental hip-hop influences.

Rick Wilhite's mix demonstrates his deep knowledge of Detroit house lineage through selections from Larry Heard and Theo Parrish, balanced with spiritual soul and disco foundations (Patti LaBelle, Giorgio Moroder, Herb Alpert). The inclusion of contemporary Detroit artists (Actress, Jerome Sydenham) alongside classic house cuts reflects his curatorial sensibility as a longtime record store proprietor. The mix emphasizes soulful depth and timeless grooves over trends.

The Brontosaurus collective centered around Phillip Lauer blends deep house foundations with minimal techno elements and disco-informed electro. The mix showcases their signature sound: rolling, funk-inflected house tracks with squelchy synths and quirky production touches. Strong Frankfurt underground aesthetic with influences from both deep house traditions and the funkier side of microhouse.

Function-curated mix representing the Sandwell District aesthetic: heavily minimalist, industrial-leaning techno with roots in Detroit and Berlin. Heavy representation from Ostgut Ton and Sandwell District releases. Mix includes acid elements and Detroit influences (Lil' Louis), progressing through hypnotic, sparse warehouse techno. Mood is deliberately austere and claustrophobic rather than euphoric.

This is a deliberately curated mix built around mood and theme rather than cohesive genre. Fever Ray assembles guitar noise, post-punk, electronics, experimental composition, and field recordings to create a Halloween-appropriate listening experience. The tracklist ranges from Neil Young's distorted guitar through Shackleton's experimental bass music to traditional Burundi percussion—unified by an unsettling, darkly introspective aesthetic rather than musical similarity.

Mix reflects Joe Goddard's stated influences: 2-step, Dutch electro, Warp material, Chicago house. Tracklist spans hip-hop (Jaylib), classic house (Detroit Emeralds, Tribe), contemporary bass music (Joy Orbison, Benga, Zed Bias), and experimental electronics (Floating Points, Four Tet remixes). Primarily vinyl-based home recording with strong emphasis on underground dance and leftfield production.

Mix centers on classic Detroit techno (Moodymann, Omar S, Kenny Larkin, Eddie Fowlkes) paired with dub techno and deep house aesthetics (Basic Channel, Levon Vincent, Kassem Mosse). Strong emphasis on vinyl sourcing and American house music heritage via Laid imprint ethos. Spiritual and introspective vibe with hypnotic, meditative qualities rather than peak-time energy.

Mix showcases Caldwell's ability to blend across multiple genres seamlessly. Tracklist features key names like Prins Thomas, Villalobos, Omar-S, Motor City Drum Ensemble, and Rui Da Silva—all signaling a sophisticated, eclectic approach rooted in house and techno but with strong soul, funk, and dub influences. The editorial emphasizes his adaptability and two-decade veteran status in Australian dance music culture.

The Matt Edwards mix 'The World of Innovative Communication' features primarily kosmische/krautrock-influenced ambient electronic music from the Innovative Communication label, with artists like Klaus Schulze and Robert Schröder dominating the tracklist. This is distinctly different from Edwards' Radio Slave techno work, emphasizing atmospheric synthesizer composition over dance floor dynamics. The withheld Rekid tracklist likely contains more sample-heavy house material based on editorial context.

Jan Krueger's mix emphasizes the slinky, hypnotic minimal house sound he's known for. Track selection from labels like Playhouse, Klang Elektronik, and Perlon reinforces the deep, introspective aesthetic typical of afterhours Berlin/international techno. The 90-minute arc builds gradually with stripped-back, sample-sparse production typical of the microhouse and minimal techno aesthetic.

Michel Baumann's mix showcases deep house fundamentals with jazz and disco influences woven throughout. The presence of classic Detroit and Chicago house (Lil Louis, GU/Cajual), Larry Heard-inspired sound design, and curated selections from his Philpot label define a refined, club-oriented approach. Mix balances exclusives with timeless samples (Babatunde Olatunji, Willie Hutch) in service of a hypnotic, sustained groove rather than peak-time intensity.

Mix blends classic funk, soul, hip-hop and boogie records with Guillaume's electronic music background. Tracklist emphasizes analog warmth and live instrumentation over pure electronic production, reflecting his stated preference for live instruments despite his move into house and techno.

Mix from François K and Derrick May (Cosmic Twins), two foundational figures in Detroit techno and house music. The editorial emphasizes their landmark status, recent studio work with Juan Atkins and others, and their continued relevance. Described as 'up-tempo' showcase of what happens when these legends collaborate.
Tensnake specializes in electronic disco with extended melodic development. Mix curated for NYE celebration with emphasis on house and disco across eras.

Mix curated by Terre Thaemlitz (DJ Sprinkles) emphasizes sparse, meditative electronic music with acoustic elements. Blends experimental electronic traditions (Oval, Deuter) with reworked pop and house references (Depeche Mode, Faye Wong remixes). The mix reflects Thaemlitz's idiosyncratic approach to electronic music—challenging structures within contemplative framework rather than dancefloor-oriented.

Marcus Worgull's trajectory from nu-jazz and broken beat to contemporary house is central to his sound. His deep knowledge as a record shop owner informs an thoughtful, curated DJ approach that balances accessibility with depth.

DJ Stingray's set reflects his deep roots in Detroit's electronic music community and his evolution toward futuristic electro. The tracklist features seminal artists from Tresor, Plus 8, and Underground Resistance—pillars of Detroit techno and experimental electronic music. Mix balances austere minimal techno with dystopian electro aesthetics and introspective soundscapes, grounded in 1990s-era underground electronic music culture.

Mix reflects The Psychonauts' aesthetic of blending electronic disco, psychedelic elements, and dreamy downtempo with curated remixes from Detroit techno pioneers (Craig, Weatherall) and contemporary electronic artists. Strong emphasis on atmospheric, non-dancefloor-oriented selections despite house influences.

Mix interlaces Jacaszek's electroacoustic compositions with modern classical masters (Pärt, Górecki) and medieval/baroque works (Bach, Tallis). Characterized by delicate textures, reverb-heavy production exploiting sacred spaces, and a contemplative aesthetic rooted in Eastern European sentimentality. Conceptually bridges electronic production and classical composition traditions.

Inspirescu is known for creating 'WTF dance floor moments' with subtle yet powerful productions. The mix showcases his range from minimal restraint to sweaty peak-time techno, recorded live at Fabric. His aesthetic combines organic, deep house with contemporary techno sensibilities rooted in the Romanian scene.

Patrick Russell's mix reflects his deep connection to Detroit and Chicago's foundational electronic music scenes. The tracklist features classic Chicago house (Fingers Inc., Logic), Detroit techno (Marcellus Pittman, Marcel Dettmann), and experimental/dub influences (Oneohtrix Point Never, Unit Moebius). His curation emphasizes spacious, meditative grooves over high-energy peaks—characteristic of his warehouse DJ background and minimal house production style.

Thompson's mix draws heavily from classic soul, funk, and jazz records with disco and house sensibilities. The tracklist emphasizes vintage deep cuts from Salsoul, Blue Thumb, and other legendary labels, combined with contemporary reinterpretations. This is sophisticated dancefloor curation rooted in the spiritual and musical lineage of American soul and Latin music, with a modern house music sensibility applied through re-edits.

A carefully curated mix showcasing Kuedo's evolution from dark industrial dubstep toward more experimental, jazz-inflected electronic music. Heavy Planet Mu presence reflects the label's experimental ethos. Mix transitions from spacious, glitchy beats to more ambient, textural territory.

Reboot's mix showcases his signature style of expertly crafted house music with emphasis on funky elements, complex rhythmic structures, and club-ready production. The tracklist features primarily house-based material with funk and soulful influences, reflecting the editorial description of 'funky' house from Frankfurt. Mix is positioned as representative of his current club DJ sets and production aesthetic.

Mix showcases Paul Rose's dual monikers: Scuba (dubstep/drum and bass focused) and SCB (four-on-the-floor techno with soulful elements). Described as 'Balearic techno with a rainy soul,' the mix bridges bass music and melodic techno, reflecting Rose's role as Hotflush Recordings label head and his connection to the UK underground and Berlin's Berghain venue.

Mix traverses from soulful organic house through dark minimal techno with emotional depth. Features classic NYC house DNA filtered through contemporary production sensibilities. Tracks like Romanthony's 'Testify' and Joe Claussell's 'Je Ka Jo' ground the set in spiritual house traditions, while selections from Emptyset and Peverelist push into darker, more experimental territory. Despite editorial mention of 'pitched-down dubstep,' no clear Dubstep tracks are evident in the tracklist.

Artist releases under alphanumeric code via Frozen Border's Horizontal Ground sub-label, emphasizing anonymity in tradition of Basic Channel and Underground Resistance. Mix reflects production style: dark, funky minimal house with emphasis on percussion and groove rather than melodic arrangement.

Fehlmann's mix reflects his legendary status bridging experimental ambient, dub techno, and Detroit-influenced minimalism. The tracklist features key collaborators (Moritz von Oswald, Omar-S, Move D) and showcases the rich, textural approach characteristic of his Kompakt work and broader artistic philosophy merging ambient, dub, and minimal house aesthetics.

Dave Huismans (2562) bridges dubstep and techno with UK garage influences. The mix heavily features his A Made Up Sound productions and collaborators from labels like Tectonic, Hemlock, and Metalheadz, showcasing a sophisticated approach to 4/4 beats layered with dubstep structural sensibilities.

Starkey's 'street bass' is a hybrid of dubstep, grime, and instrumental hip-hop with UK garage foundations. The mix blends dub-heavy remixes, grime artists (Terror Danjah, Scratcha DVA), bass music staples (Kaiser, Spooky), and hip-hop tracks into a cohesive, energetic DJ set that showcases his Philly interpretation of UK bass culture.

Fudge Fingas curates a measured deep house session anchored by Firecracker and Prime Numbers material, blending soulful house grooves with jazzy melodies and organic textures. The mix opens with Weldon Irvine's soul foundation and closes with Sun Ra's cosmic outro, creating a contemplative late-night arc. Tracks from artists like Andy Stott and KiNK add minimal techno undertones while maintaining the core deep house aesthetic that defines the Firecracker collective's Edinburgh sound.

Mike Slott is primarily a hip-hop producer working in experimental and glitchy styles, influenced by the LuckyMe collective's forward-thinking approach. His work combines affecting melodies with unconventional, off-kilter beat construction rather than pure club music.

Rub N Tug are NYC disco purists pivoting toward housey sounds. Their background in underground disco parties combined with contemporary house production creates a sophisticated blend. The mix likely features soulful, groove-oriented house with disco undertones, consistent with their reputation for party dynamics and their work reinterpreting classic artists.

Leonel Castillo's mix emphasizes his varied house and techno productions from Buenos Aires. The editorial highlights his influence from early acid and ambient techno, with releases on respected labels like Ilian Tape. The podcast features primarily unreleased material, reflecting his deep connection to the local scene rather than commercial success.

Marcel Fengler's mix showcases his signature approach: refined atmospheric techno with dub textures, strategic dynamic arcs, and deep underground sensibility. Tracks from Autechre, Silent Servant, Marcel Dettmann, and Carl Craig anchor a carefully constructed journey through minimal techno, dub techno, and broken-beat elements. The editorial emphasizes his mastery of building tension and varying intensity across a set—'snaking through the night in waves.'

This Tigersushi curated mix blends electronic dance music with art-rock and experimental sensibilities. The tracklist spans classic house, techno, and electro with deliberate detours into experimental electronics (Autechre, Excepter) and leftfield choices. Reflects the label's signature approach of merging dance and rock influences. The presence of both James Holden (techno), John Tejada (minimal), and Skream (dubstep) alongside Toto, Lucio Battisti, and Etienne Daho demonstrates the brothers' eclectic, genre-fluid approach to DJing.

Jaar's mix emphasizes thoughtful electronic composition with classical training evident. Wolf + Lamb connection indicates deep house lineage but with more experimental, ambient-leaning sensibility than typical house. The described 'metronomic' quality and modern-classical background suggest microhouse and ambient techno territories.

Elektro Guzzi uniquely blend rock instrumentation (guitar, bass, drums) with techno and Krautrock sensibilities. This RA podcast captures a live set performed as one continuous groove with no overdubs or laptops, emphasizing their reputation as a live act. The influence of noise, dub, and jazz from members' previous work adds textural complexity to their unconventional approach to 4/4 dance music.

Fred P is a Queens-based deep house producer influenced by NYC's classic house music era (Red Zone, Sound Factory, Tunnel). His sound emphasizes introspection and melodic sophistication over the harder styles associated with New York house, with emphasis on shuffling percussion and emotional resonance.

Tevo Howard represents contemporary Chicago house music with strong melodic sensibilities and live instrumentation. The presence of his father's blues vocals on some tracks adds an earthy, organic quality to the electronic foundation. This is a live performance session rather than a studio mix.

Magda curates a summer afterparty mix drawing from disco, post-punk, early electro and minimal techno. The selection spans Lindstrom's melodic minimalism, Black Devil Disco Club's funky disco-house, Cybotron's Detroit electro, and post-punk touchstones like Chris and Cosey and Grauzone, unified by a sophisticated, dancefloor-focused sensibility rather than a single genre.

Mount Kimbie's mix showcases their signature blend of fractured UK bass music, glitchy electronic production, and atmospheric soundscapes. The inclusion of artists like James Blake, Xela, and Actress reflects the contemporary leftfield electronic scene they inhabit. Recorded in a home setting with tape machines and field recordings, the mix emphasizes texture and mood over dancefloor functionality, with an emphasis on unconventional song structures and subtle melodic development.

Jef K is a veteran Parisian DJ known for deep, infectious bassline-led house music rooted in UK garage influences. The mix reflects his respected status in European underground house culture, with emphasis on craftsmanship and dancefloor sophistication rather than commercial appeal.

Peverelist is known for fusing dub reggae aesthetics with techno and jungle influences, creating a distinctive Bristol sound centered on soundsystem culture. The mix features dubplates cut specifically for live DJ sets, emphasizing vinyl culture and deep, percussive techno with heavy dub and jungle elements.

The mix showcases Cio D'Or's signature blend of hypnotic minimal techno with tripped-out ambient passages. Heavy presence of Monolake, Milton Bradley, Xhin, and other Berlin/Cologne school minimal and dub techno artists. The tracklist arc moves from introspective ambient (Murcof, Margaret Dygas) into deeper minimal techno grooves, then back toward ambient textures. Multiple versions of 'Goldbrokat' at the end (Donato Dozzy remixes spanning techno to ambient) perfectly encapsulate the mix's duality.

Harri and Domenic are legendary Glasgow DJs with 16+ years at Sub Club's Saturday residency, blending house and techno with deep knowledge of the 1980s-90s electronic music explosion. Their style emphasizes warmth and soul over pure dancefloor intensity, reflecting their roots in house music's golden era.

Shed's mix showcases his signature blend of classic Detroit and Berlin techno with contemporary breakbeat, dubstep, and ambient elements. Features key artists from Ostgut Ton, Hard Wax, Hemlock, and Hotflush—bastions of European underground techno. The tracklist balances driving techno with experimental, textural elements, reflecting Pawlowitz's 'oblique rhythms and coherent diversity.'

Steven Ford's Bruno Pronsato project emphasizes minimalist techno with dancefloor sensibility, rooted in Berlin's electronic music scene. The live set format and description of 'groovy minimalist style' suggest stripped-back, rhythmically-focused techno rather than ambient or experimental work.

Superpitcher's 'voodoo' framing emphasizes the spiritual, mystical quality of minimal techno rather than literal voodoo music. The mix blends micro house foundations with melodic and organic sensibilities, featuring key Kompakt artists and associates (DJ Koze, Roman Flügel, Isoleé). Editorial context confirms Schaufler's versatility across schaffel, microhouse, and emotive electronic pop, with this mix showcasing the introspective, stripped-down aesthetic that defines contemporary Cologne minimalism.

Mix explicitly positioned as end-of-summer soundtrack with minimal techno despite Berlin residency. Heavy emphasis on disco and house with funk/soul sensibility, featuring exclusive edits of classic soul/R&B artists.

Vintage 1993 mixtape showcasing Scott Grooves' Detroit house roots with deep, soulful production influenced by jazz and funk. Features classic remixes from the early house era (MK, Tony Humphries, CJ Mackintosh). The mix balances accessibility with technical sophistication characteristic of Detroit's electronic music tradition.

Kowton deliberately fuses dubstep and techno aesthetics, blending reduced tempos with heavily swung rhythms and deep sub-bass. The mix features key UK bass and experimental electronic producers (D-Bridge, Peverelist, Shed, Svreca) alongside unreleased material, creating a cohesive yet forward-thinking selection that defies strict genre categorization.

Detroit Beatdown represents a unique fusion of house and techno sensibilities rooted in Motor City tradition. The tracklist shows deep house influences (Ralf GUM, Kerri Chandler remixes) mixed with stripped-back, percussive grooves characteristic of Detroit's minimalist approach. Three separate sets showcase individual styles while maintaining cohesive aesthetic—raw, groove-oriented, and introspective rather than flashy.

Mix reflects Smagghe's older school eclecticism—unafraid of wild tempo shifts across house, techno, and experimental electronic. Tracks span labels like Kill The DJ, Kompakt, Numbers, and Clone; many are remixes by respected producers (Alex Smoke, Optimo). Editorial context emphasizes his Rough Trade education and open-minded sensibility. Set spans old and new, suitable for peak-time club play.

Commix (drum and bass duo) deliberately subvert expectations by presenting a house and techno mix. Tracklist features minimal/ambient techno artists (Sigha, STL, Wax, Photek) with deep underground label affiliations (Hotflush, Smallville, Fachwerk). Mix designed as a sonic journey across 4/4 genres, reflecting the duo's philosophy of avoiding insularity and exploring diverse musical territories.

Mix draws from Alan Howarth's extensive film scoring work with John Carpenter and others, emphasizing eerie, unstable soundscapes and dark ambient textures rather than dancefloor-oriented electronic music.

Space Dimension Controller's mix emphasizes vintage electro and funk with a contemporary techno sensibility. The 'time-traveling producer' conceit reflects the actual production style: retro-tinged sounds with modern production clarity. Mix blends his own original tracks with curated old-school selections, suggesting an eclectic but cohesive underground club aesthetic.

Mix blends classic Detroit/Chicago house and techno with contemporary acid sounds and breakbeat elements. KiNK's selection reflects his stated philosophy of 'old and new, harsh and mellow, straight and broken.' Vinyl-sourced alongside digital, recorded in software aptly named 'ACID.' Tracklist includes foundational figures (Jeff Mills, Autechre) mixed with contemporary Rush Hour releases, suggesting lineage-aware but forward-thinking approach.

ASC and the Autonomic collective have carved a distinct niche within drum and bass by halving typical tempos and embracing ambient, textural production. This mix showcases 'liquid funkstep' and atmospheric DnB with strong IDM influences—ethereal pads, abstract soundscapes, and intricate but understated rhythms define the aesthetic.

Terror Danjah is a godfather figure in grime who pushes beyond genre boundaries. This mix blends grime, dubstep, garage, drum and bass, and dub with an emphasis on instrumental explorations and forward-thinking production. The tracklist features collaborations with key scene figures (D Double E, D.O.K, P Jam) and releases from seminal labels (Hyperdub, Planet Mu, Butterz, Hardrive). The editorial emphasizes the genre-fluid nature of his sound.

jozif's approach emphasizes melodic lines and organic acoustic drum sounds within a house framework. The mix showcases his role as a London in-demand DJ balancing dancefloor appeal with sophisticated production sensibilities. Exclusively remixes and edits highlight his remix skills and contemporary house aesthetic of the early 2010s.

Mix showcases Dundov's signature melodically rich approach, straddling house and techno. Features artists aligned with minimal and deep techno aesthetics (Echospace, Claude Young, Deetron). The journey builds from introspective ambient-tinged elements toward a resplendent conclusion, reflecting Dundov's multi-decade career working with imprints like Music Man Records and his parallel ambient practice.

Perc constructs a mix rooted in industrial and experimental electronic music (Throbbing Gristle, Cabaret Voltaire, Nitzer Ebb) that feeds into contemporary industrial techno. The progression moves from abstract/noise elements toward driving, rhythmic techno that maintains a dark, cerebral edge throughout. Heavy use of remixes and edits emphasizes the 'turning genre inside out' approach mentioned in the blurb.

Ben UFO curates a deliberately unclassifiable year-end summary reflecting Hessle Audio's ethos of connecting disparate electronic music genres. Mix emphasizes dialogue between house, techno, UK funky, garage, grime, and dubstep variants. Recorded mostly on vinyl via Technics turntables, capturing the label's approach to transcending genre boundaries in UK club music circa 2010.

Tiger & Woods emphasize creative editing and sample manipulation across soul, disco, boogie and house. The mix reflects their 'groove extraction' studio methodology with treated, dubbed-on-cassette material, creating a nostalgic yet contemporary feel-good aesthetic.

Mathew Jonson is known for deep, minimal techno with strong melodic elements and meticulous live hardware manipulation. The Labyrinth 2010 set represents his festival-appropriate style—driving yet sophisticated, building tension through layered synthesis rather than brute force.

Mix showcases contemporary London house with emphasis on grooving, melodic elements and understated production. Blends Deep House sensibilities with Tech House's structural precision. Notable use of edits and unreleased tracks demonstrates curator's insider knowledge of the scene.

Mix showcases Catz N' Dogz's broad house and techno interests across multiple eras and labels (DirtyBird, Crosstown Rebels, Mothership, etc.). Tracklist spans from ambient textures (Out Hud) through deep house and minimal techno cuts to more peak-time elements, reflecting their stated approach of raiding their entire record collection for variety and depth.

Jonas Kopp's mix blends minimal techno with deeper house sensibilities, rooted in Berlin and Detroit traditions but filtered through an Argentine producer's lens. The tracklist emphasizes atmospheric, stripped-back productions on labels like Ostgut Ton and Stroboscopic Artefacts, with steady momentum suitable for mid-set club play rather than peak-time energy.

Isolee's first podcast is a curated listening experience rather than a DJ set, emphasizing his signature microhouse aesthetic alongside house, electronica, and soul influences. The vinyl-to-Ableton approach reflects his meticulous producer mindset applied to curation.

Adam X (Traversable Wormhole) curates a masterclass in unrelenting industrial techno that bridges his 90s EBM roots with contemporary hard techno. The mix features key CLR releases, Sonic Groove catalog deep cuts, and collaborators like Surgeon and Marcel Dettmann. Murky, atmospheric openings give way to increasingly hostile and mechanical soundscapes—this is conscious resistance to the minimal/microhouse trends that dominated the 2000s.

Caribou's mix showcases his exploratory approach to electronic music post-Swim album success. Heavy presence of Daphni aliases (5 exclusive tracks) demonstrates his interest in stripped-back, detailed electronic production. Mix blends vinyl sources with digital tracks and original material, spanning experimental IDM aesthetics (Lone, Ricardo Villalobos), world music samples (Charanjit Singh, Thomas Mapfumo), and glitchy microhouse textures. Overall character is introspective and studio-focused rather than club-oriented, reflecting Snaith's indie-electronic crossover position.

Pinch's mix showcases the range of dubstep's origins and evolution, opening with ambient and dub techno influences (Eno, Rhythm & Sound, Rau) before building intensity toward harder, bass-focused dubstep tracks. The editorial notes emphasize meditative qualities and a deliberate structural arc that mirrors a night out—beginning with introspective, textural elements and culminating in energetic, club-ready material. Key dubstep institutions (Tectonic, Swamp 81, Rinse/Dubplate culture) and collaborators are heavily featured.

Danny Tenaglia's mix reflects his legendary status in NYC house music, drawing from Paradise Garage and the late '90s minimal house aesthetic. The tracklist features contemporary producers working in deep, hypnotic tech-house territory with tribal and percussive elements. The deeply hypnotic, groove-centric approach is characteristic of Tenaglia's signature rolling sound.

A curated journey through diverse styles—classical, ambient, experimental electronic, and alternative pop—that subverts traditional genre boundaries. The mix prioritizes texture, mood, and artistic vision over dancefloor functionality, reflecting Discodeine's position as thoughtful producers interested in cross-genre aesthetics rather than strict club sound.

Heinrich Mueller's first mix showcases his interconnected project ecosystem (Dopplereffekt, Zwischenwelt, Der Zyklus, Arpanet, ArD2) with dense, cerebral electro rooted in Detroit's legacy. The tracklist emphasizes abstract scientific/philosophical concepts and advanced sound design rather than conventional DJ mixing. Slow-burn journey through industrial and experimental electronic territories.

Mix bridges house and techno through a decidedly funky, jazz-informed lens. Recloose's Detroit roots (Planet E connection) evident throughout, with emphasis on musicality over stripped-down minimalism. Includes soul and funk touchstones alongside contemporary producers. A thoughtful, groove-oriented mix that prioritizes hooks and instrumental sophistication.

Marco Carola's mix emphasizes minimal, groove-oriented techno with stripped-back production. The tracklist features artists from his network (Minus, Metroline Limited, Cadenza) and reflects his documented shift from frantic multi-deck mixing to a more spacious, rhythmically focused approach. Editorial context confirms deliberate warmth and breathing room over typical peak-time intensity.

Mix reflects Adam Marshall's signature style: emotional depth paired with raw, earthy production. Heavy representation from his New Kanada label roster and like-minded producers. Emphasis on melody within minimal/deep frameworks. Recorded on vinyl with Ableton, suggesting hybrid analog-digital approach. Canadian producer working in Berlin scene context.

Mix showcases Maceo Plex's 'fun' side with slow-burning house tracks that maintain dancefloor appeal. Editorial emphasizes the low-slung, sensual aesthetic with touches of disco and electro. Recorded in studio with Traktor and digital vinyl control.

Lucy's mix exemplifies the Stroboscopic Artefacts aesthetic: a sophisticated exploration of shadows between techno, dub, and ambient. Tracks from Shackleton, Boards of Canada, and Forward Strategy Group emphasize texture over pulse, while Berlin remixes (Dettmann, Dozzy) anchor the sound. This is introspective peak-time music—hypnotic rather than euphoric, with emphasis on production detail and emotional depth rather than dancefloor immediacy.

Mix prioritizes elegance and grace over dancefloor utility. Heavy emphasis on hip-hop, pop, and chill wave as stated in blurb, with spoken word and oddities woven throughout. Recorded in bedroom/kitchen setting on turntable and vinyl, creating deliberately intimate and non-professional production aesthetic that aligns with 'Good Morning' concept—a slow-burn, home-listening experience rather than peak-time DJ set.

O/V/R combines Ruskin's Blueprint lineage with Regis's Downwards/Sandwell District aesthetic—a fusion of techno rigor with post-punk bleakness. The live Berlin setting and equipment-based approach creates dynamic tension between control and chaos, resulting in music that is simultaneously minimal and foreboding.

Curated retrospective of foundational late '80s house from the original Trax, Warehouse, and Dance Mania labels. Emphasizes the jacking grooves and acid synth lines that define the Chicago-Detroit sound. Steve Bug's mix exemplifies Poker Flat's stated mission to honor early house music origins while maintaining contemporary DJ sensibility.

Skream mix centered on dubstep's golden era with heavy UK grime and garage influences. Tracklist dominated by Skream productions, collaborations with Benga/Magnetic Man, and contemporaries (Dismantle, Caspa). Club-focused with prominent basslines and UK MCs (Trim, Donaeo). Mix includes some crossover tracks (The Weeknd, Prince) but primarily showcases underground UK electronic music culture.

Mix features live sequencing and vocal elements, positioned between accessible house and more experimental techno with organic, melodic sensibilities. References to Pronsato and Villalobos suggest sophisticated, leftfield house music with artistic depth.

Mix spans the breadth of UK bass music with significant Afrobeats/Southern African influences (Black Motion, DJ Shifty, DJ Sdoko), jungle/drum and bass ancestry (Omni Trio), and contemporary club sounds. LV's Hyperdub and Keysound affiliations evident in the leftfield, genre-blending approach. Editorial describes it as diverse with 'digital dub to kwaito-infused 2-step' lineage. The tracklist reveals deep-cut sourcing, heavy remix culture presence, and emphasis on rhythmic experimentation over purist genre adherence.

Mix showcases Sei A's signature blend of 4/4 club styles with a distinctive bass music sensibility (Mount Kimbie-influenced rather than grime-oriented). The 65-minute selection emphasizes subtle production, afterhours aesthetics, and emotional restraint over peak-time intensity. Multiple Turbo Recordings releases indicate the label as a key outlet for this sound.

Marcelus (Cédric Bros) is a Paris-based producer whose style draws from Berghain's house-leaning residents but incorporates acid elements (evident in tracks like 'Sulfuric'). The mix balances atmospheric house with gritty techno over vinyl, recorded with Xone:92. A debut artist showing both production and DJ mastery across a young catalogue.

Mix reflects Tobi Neumann's established position in Berlin's house scene, drawing from Cocoon-affiliated aesthetics and featuring key labels like Cadenza, Pampa, and FXHE. The selection balances minimal tech house foundations with deeper, groove-oriented house, showcasing the matured sound of German house culture that emerged from the early 2000s minimal movement.

Convextion is known for strict adherence to techno and electro; the live set emphasizes his trademark fast-paced approach within these core genres. The artist's deliberate anonymity and limited output are defining characteristics of his legacy.

Planningtorock's DJ approach emphasizes experimental electronic music with art-house sensibilities, featuring mashed-up edits, remixes of indie/alternative tracks (Radiohead, Grimes), and collaborations with experimental peers. The mix prioritizes texture, voice manipulation, and compositional complexity over dancefloor functionality, reflecting her classical training and conceptual approach to electronic music.

DJ Qu's mix embodies the NYC underground quality sound, featuring unreleased tracks from key artists (Fred P, Levon Vincent, Jus-Ed) alongside polished deep house productions. The emphasis on warmth, groove, and emotional depth rather than peak-time energy defines this as contemplative rather than club-focused.

Mix traces house and techno's development through key artists (Jeff Mills, Mr. Fingers, Photek) while showcasing Davenport's contemporary refined approach to the genres. Blends classic Detroit and Chicago house foundations with modern European minimal techno sensibilities.

Tornado Wallace specializes in slow-mo house with swirling organ textures and disco loops. The mix features classic soul and funk foundations (Tata Vega, Herbie Hancock, Lee Ritenour) remixed and recontextualized through modern house sensibilities. Deliberately measured energy with emphasis on melodic development rather than peak-time intensity. Strong emphasis on dubbed-out, atmospheric production.

Tokimonsta's mix draws heavily from the Low End Theory LA beats community, blending chopped hip-hop samples with IDM-influenced production and glitchy textures. The aesthetic is deliberately moody and intricate rather than high-energy, featuring artists like Flying Lotus, Samiyam, and Daedelus who prioritize compositional sophistication over dancefloor functionality.

Mix blends classic Chicago house (Paul Johnson, Tyree, Dance Mania records) with contemporary bass-heavy house from Dirtybird and affiliated labels. Strong funk and soul samples throughout, with VonStroke's signature approach of combining serious musicianship with playful production touches. Tracklist spans Dance Mania classics through contemporary tech-house and club-ready bass music.

Mix emphasizes emotional depth and production refinement over dancefloor energy. Artists like Theo Parrish, Omar-S, and DJ Qu represent the thoughtful, soulful end of house and techno. The editorial context highlights Mosse's commitment to emotional connection and sonic depth rather than trend-chasing.

Renaissance Man's mix prioritizes sonic experimentation and genre eclecticism over adherence to specific styles. The tracklist reflects diverse influences from electro, breakbeat, and abstract techno, unified by an experimental ethos. The editorial context emphasizes their use of unconventional samples (ticking clocks, spray cans, religious chants, sitars) and rejection of rigid genre conventions.

FitzGerald bridges house, garage, and bass music traditions with an emphasis on unreleased Hotflush material. The mix blends melodic elements with glitchy, minimal production—reflective of the Man Make Music ethos of genre fluidity. Artists like Sigha, Appleblim, and Scuba contribute textural, atmospheric contributions alongside more groove-oriented UK garage-influenced tracks. Primarily instrumental with emphasis on production detail and atmospheric depth.

Mix bridges vintage Detroit techno and NYC/Chicago house traditions through contemporary European techno lens. Strong presence of Deeply Rooted House label releases alongside Berlin-school techno (Dettmann, Klock, Dehnert). Predominantly instrumental, sophisticated production focus.

A deeply experimental mix that bridges minimal techno, glitch, and ambient territories. Alva Noto curates work from fellow Raster-Noton pioneers and complementary artists, emphasizing process-driven composition, microscopic sound design, and conceptual rigor over dancefloor energy. The inclusion of Steve Reich and Angelo Badalamenti signals engagement with both minimalist classical and cinematic composition. This is curator-as-artist territory—intellectual, uncompromising, and aesthetically unified despite surface diversity.

Cosmin TRG's style is defined by a unique fusion of techno with bass music elements from his dubstep/garage background. The mix represents a producer comfortable moving between genres while maintaining a cohesive sonic identity—techno as the primary framework but filtered through low-frequency bass music sensibilities.

Mix showcases foundational deep house and Detroit techno records that shaped the genre. Huckaby's selection reflects his dual role as both respected producer and music educator, featuring seminal works from Detroit (Moodymann, Rhythm is Rhythm), Chicago (Lil Louis), and European pioneers. The tracklist emphasizes soulful, synthesis-driven house music with strong emphasis on depth and spiritual resonance rather than peak-time energy.

Mix features a curated blend of classic rock, electronic pioneers, and contemporary artists that evoke the Balearic aesthetic—mixing Krautrock (Neu!, Tangerine Dream), psychedelic rock (Pink Floyd, The Byrds), and atmospheric electronic music to create a nostalgic, contemplative journey reminiscent of Ibiza's sunset culture.

Martyn is explicitly positioned as a genre-bender who pioneered connections between UK bass music and Detroit/Berlin techno. His versatility is demonstrated by seamlessly playing both bass music venues (Sub:stance) and house music clubs (Panorama Bar). Second album described as return to Chicago and Detroit roots.

labels: blend of club and art music
notes: Mix emphasizes melodic and harmonic content within techno frameworks, with significant glitch and IDM influences. Features collaborators and remixes alongside unreleased material, showcasing Cooper's taste for sonically adventurous artists like Tim Hecker, Ben Frost, and Amon Tobin. The palette bridges dancefloor techno with more experimental, cinematic electronic music.

Mix emphasizes droning 4/4 rhythms with dark ambient and modern classical textures. Features experimental remixes and obscure electronic artists exploring industrial and minimalist soundscapes. Strong conceptual framework reflecting existential themes.

A carefully curated selection that transcends typical club fare. While rooted in deep house and melodic techno, the mix emphasizes emotional depth and textural subtlety over dancefloor intensity. Featuring remixes and production from UK and European electronic luminaries, the selection showcases Tale of Us' sophistication as curators and their ability to move beyond peak-time DJ conventions.

Mix showcases Dexter's decade-long electro vision, bridging Dutch production heritage with UK bass innovation and Detroit techno foundations. Heavy presence of dub remixes and warped vocal treatments. Artists like Drexciya, Dopplereffekt, and The Clone Machine signal deep futuristic/dystopian themes alongside contemporary UK producers (Boddika, Untold, Bok Bok). Tracklist emphasizes kinetic energy and hyper-detailed production rather than straightforward house or techno—distinctly electro in the contemporary sense.

Mix reflects Friendly Fires' dual identity as both a band and club-oriented selectors. Tracks by Maceo Plex, Roman Flügel, Michael Mayer and Matthew Styles indicate a preference for deep, melodic techno and house with sophisticated production. The tracklist skews toward modern club music with accessible hooks rather than purely underground techno.

The mix reflects Xhin's evolution toward a fusion of techno fundamentals with IDM's intricate, abstract sensibilities. Tracks from Autechre, Plaid remixes, and artists on Stroboscopic Artefacts demonstrate a shift toward more experimental, less dancefloor-oriented electronic music. The blurb emphasizes the 'weighty' and unconventional nature of his recent work, distinguishing it from standard house/techno releases.

Red D (Bart Van Neste) runs We Play House label with clear house music agenda. Mix features contemporary house and deep techno from Dekmantel, L.I.E.S., and other forward-thinking labels. Terrence Parker and Luv Jam selections anchor the house foundation, while Max Cooper and Martyn add techno sophistication. Overall aesthetic is refined, soulful, and rooted in European underground club culture.

Consequence's approach blends the experimental, sample-rich aesthetics of IDM artists like Aphex Twin with advanced drum and bass production. The mix emphasizes the more club-oriented side of his work while maintaining the melodic sophistication that defines the Autonomic aesthetic pioneered by dBridge and Instra:mental.

Mix showcases Jackmaster's signature inclusive approach spanning Detroit techno (Underground Resistance), UK bass (Zed Bias, Joy Orbison), Baltimore club (Playground Productions), house music, and drum and bass. The sequencing prioritizes dancefloor energy and accessibility rather than strict genre coherence, reflecting his reputation as a party-friendly DJ with deep record-store knowledge. Strong presence of unreleased tracks and dubplates indicates curation from his close network on the Numbers label.

Mix showcases contemporary minimal and deep house aesthetics from early 2010s, with emphasis on textural, understated production. Tracks span Tech House (Worthy & Eats Everything), Detroit-influenced minimal (Omar S, Fred P), and sophisticated deep house (Roman Flugel's own work). The editorial context emphasizes Flugel's 2011 artistic peak and the curation reflects a DJ at peak listening awareness rather than dancefloor functionality—a curator's mix.

Lee Foss's mix reflects his 90s hip-hop/R&B influenced house sound that defined Hot Natured/Hot Creations. Tracklist features contemporary house artists (SBTRKT, Daphni, Nina Kraviz, Midland) with soulful, tech-house aesthetic. Mix recorded during touring in late 2011, embodying the era's peak-time dancefloor sensibility.

TR-101 is a rare live collaboration between two Berlin techno veterans (DJ Pete/Substance and Sleeparchive). The mix emphasizes stripped-down, dark minimal techno performed on hardware synthesizers and equipment (TR-101 reference). Recorded at Berghain in October 2011, this captures the refined, pitch-black sound of contemporary Berlin techno.

Nicholas delivers a house-centric live set rooted in Chicago house traditions and soul sampling, emphasizing his original productions and remixes. The mix showcases deep, soulful house with strong lyrical themes of love and spirituality, recorded live at home with laptop and MIDI controller.

Mix showcases DJ Nobu's mastery of minimal and Berlin-style techno with influences from Detroit. Tracklist features key artists from the minimal techno canon (Cosmin TRG, Donato Dozzy, Surgeon, Sandwell District, Marcel Dettmann). The editorial context emphasizes dark, angular driving 4/4 constructed with expert curation. Recorded on analog and digital hardware in his studio, reflecting a hybrid production approach.

Jay Shepheard's mix centers on house music with a strong disco thread throughout. The tracklist features primarily deep house and soulful house tracks with disco elements, including remixes and edits that emphasize groove and danceability. The editorial context emphasizes his consistent artistic vision and avoidance of trends, reflected in a mature, quality-focused selection.

Mix heavily features classic Detroit and Chicago house/techno with acid elements (DJ Sneak, Floorplan, Omar S). Interspersed with spoken word and psychedelic breaks (Pink Floyd, Moody Blues). Reflects Kraviz's philosophy of loose, organic mixing with deep dug vinyl selections from the underground.

Synkro's sound defies easy categorization—a sophisticated blend of fast breakbeats (drum & bass/dubstep territory around 130 BPM) with deep emotive atmospherics and ambient textures. The mix showcases influences from IDM pioneers (Boards of Canada, Autechre) while maintaining a grounded, warm aesthetic. Tracks emphasize production craft and emotional resonance over peak-time energy, reflecting his diverse output across multiple sub-genres and labels.

Patrice Scott's mix showcases the Detroit house tradition with soaring pads, delicate keys, and depth characteristic of his Sistrum Recordings output. The tracklist blends Detroit techno roots with house sensibilities, featuring dub mixes and remixes that emphasize melody and emotional resonance over pure dancefloor aggression.

A decade-spanning retrospective that weaves together microhouse, ambient techno, and deep house through meticulous DJ craft. Devaud's signature style emphasizes melodic sophistication and atmospheric depth over peak-time energy, drawing from contemporary electronic experimentalists (Burial, Floating Points, Gas, Fennesz) alongside house and techno foundations (Omar S, Plastikman, Ricardo Villalobos). The mix reflects his jazz influence and studio producer background, creating a cohesive introspective journey rather than a conventional dance floor narrative.

DJ Harvey is renowned for his sophisticated, soulful approach to house music with emphasis on edits and reissues. The mix reflects his legendary status as a tastemaker and his ability to craft extended journeys across disco, house, and deep house influences.

Floating Points is characterized by a sophisticated fusion of jazz composition with house and techno, drawing from his piano background and London club culture. The mix emphasizes soul, disco, and bass elements woven through club frameworks—languid tempos with deep harmonic sensibility.

Developer's Modularz-centered mix showcases stripped-back, meditative techno with emphasis on rhythm manipulation and atmospheric depth. Features recurring collaborators (Silent Servant, Abdulla Rashim, Eduardo De La Calle) creating marked sonic coherence. The editorial context emphasizes his 'dusky, tunnel-like' aesthetic and comfort with off-beat dynamics—suggesting industrial and dub techno influences alongside core minimal techno.

A Guy Called Gerald is a foundational figure in acid house and jungle. This mix centers on classic acid house tracks from his collection, with emphasis on TB-303 synthesis—the core sound that defined 'Voodoo Ray' and his broader influence on electronic music.

Airhead's mix balances ambient, glitchy textures with selective moments of percussive pressure. The aesthetic is defined by sparse arrangements and emotional restraint rather than high energy—influenced by James Blake's approach but distinct. Draws from UK electronic underground with influences from grime (Wiley) and UK garage heritage.

Chez Damier is a foundational figure in house and techno; this mix likely draws from his classic era work with KMS and Prescription label, representing peak house music innovation from the late 80s-90s
