Matthew Dear curates a mix suspended between eras, emphasizing minimal and Detroit techno fundamentals with raw house influences. The selection from artists like DJ Bone, Planetary Assault Systems, and Radio Slave creates a cohesive underground narrative that avoids contemporary trends, favoring timeless dancefloor sophistication.

DjRUM constructs a non-linear narrative mix using natural soundscapes (Chris Watson field recordings), jazz standards, classical influences, and his own experimental productions. The mix prioritizes mood and cinematic arc over dancefloor functionality, blending breakcore/drum and bass foundations with ambient, dub, and IDM aesthetics.

Woolford's evolution from jackin' Chicago house to darker, more introspective minimal techno and deep house. Mix balances Detroit influence (Mr Fingers, Carl Craig connection) with UK bass-inflected experimentation. Tracklist features contemporary minimal techno innovators (Kangding Ray, Actress, Dario Zenker) alongside house purists, showcasing Woolford's position as a bridge between classic house traditions and contemporary underground techno.

Mix curated by Erik K Skodvin (Svarte Greiner/Deaf Center co-founder). Tracklist emphasizes dark ambient, contemporary classical, and experimental electronics with strong emphasis on atonal, dissonant, and atmospheric textures. Notable artists include Burzum, Volcano the Bear, Future Sound of London, and Valerio Tricoli. The mix prioritizes challenging, non-commercial aesthetic over accessibility.

Mix curated by Shifted, an anonymous artist with prior career success in another genre who has become a key voice in contemporary techno. Tracklist spans established classics (ESP, Damon Wild) with cutting-edge contemporary producers (ROD, Rødhåd, Markus Suckut). Heavy emphasis on textural complexity, industrial elements, and minimalist approaches characteristic of recent Berlin-influenced and UK underground techno scenes.

Vakula's mix is entirely self-curated from his own unreleased and forthcoming material. The editorial context emphasizes his deep engagement with classic US house traditions (Detroit/Chicago) while maintaining an unexpected, eclectic approach through unconventional samples (organ, gospel), extended track lengths, and atmospheric depth. The mood is contemplative and meditative rather than peak-time club energy.

Deetron crafts a sophisticated 70+ minute journey blending classic house/techno foundations (Kraftwerk, Marvin Gaye samples) with contemporary deep and minimal techno. The mix showcases his reputation for elegant three-deck mixing, balancing established classics (Roy Ayers, Jeff Mills, Todd Terje) with remixes and deep cuts from quality labels (Hessle Audio, Warp, Rekids). The tone is relentless yet refined—high-energy club music with intellectual underpinnings, neither peak-time bangers nor introspective ambient, but rather a masterclass in DJ curation that validates his mid-90s longevity in house and techno.

Mix exists at the intersection of dubstep and drum & bass, drawing heavily from '90s golden age influences (Source Direct, Photek) but filtered through a distinctly Tokyo perspective. Strong emphasis on dub versions and skeletal production. Recorded with Traktor and vinyl controller for an 'old school feeling.' Largely consists of unreleased material from upcoming 7even Recordings album.
Mix emphasizes McPhee's DIY production philosophy with deliberately imperfect, human-touched sound design. No tracklist provided, featuring mostly unreleased originals and collaborations.

Kerri Chandler is a canonical figure in deep house and East Coast house music. This mix represents his warm-up aesthetic for a 2012 festival setting, emphasizing his ability to build hypnotic, groove-oriented house tracks with soul and sophistication.

Mix emphasizes bass, breaks, and groove-oriented house music with strong Detroit and UK garage influences. Eats Everything's signature style blends tech house elements with soulful, mellow atmospherics over extended duration.

Mix showcases Benoit & Sergio's curated taste spanning classic house (Frankie Knuckles), contemporary deep house, and electronic pop influences. Tracklist emphasizes warm, melodic production with vocal elements and a distinctly summery, uplifting mood. The selection reflects their editorial taste for songs with hooks and singalong qualities while maintaining dancefloor credibility.

Mix emphasizes texture, space, and feel over beat-driven energy. Heavy on Rrose's own material alongside avant-garde producers and experimental techno artists. Strong presence of glitchy, industrial, and ambient elements. Tracks from Eaux label previewed. Blends club-oriented tracks with abstract sound art and electroacoustic experimentation.

Mix demonstrates Zabiela's signature approach of technologically-enhanced hip-hop aesthetics applied to house and breakbeats. The three-month production timeline reflects meticulous attention to sample curation, track selection, and emotional narrative. Strong presence of leftfield electronic music (Warp, Planet Mu, Ninja Tune releases) balanced with accessible melodic elements. The interpolated spoken-word samples and field recordings create a contemplative, introspective mood throughout.

This mix is fundamentally defined by the 'screwed' production technique (slowed, pitch-shifted, often distorted) applied across diverse source material including house, R&B, footwork, and experimental electronics. While 'Chopped and Screwed' isn't in the provided vocabulary, it's the core identity of this mix and essential to accurate classification. The editorial context emphasizes Shlohmo's fascination with late-90s/early-00s screwed music (DJ Screw, Swishahouse) combined with contemporary LA beat scene, hip-hop, and shoegaze influences.

Mix blends classic soul and funk records with modern deep house and techno, reflecting Mister Saturday Night's aesthetic of bridging eras and moods. The selection spans Roland Kirk and Jill Scott through Anthony Naples and Anthony Shake Shakir, creating a narrative arc from introspective soul through groovy house. Perfect for the party's outdoor Sunday afternoon Brooklyn events.

A carefully curated ambient/drone mix emphasizing sustained tones, processed guitars, and minimalist composition. The tracklist spans foundational ambient figures (Harold Budd, Cluster & Eno) through modern practitioners (Eluvium, Alva Noto), reflecting The Sight Below's Gas-influenced aesthetic and the Seattle-based experimental community's ethos. Distinctly non-dance, introspective electronic music designed for quiet, solitary listening.

tINI's signature style emphasizes deep, minimal house with strong percussive elements and organic textures. The mix reflects her Desolat affiliation and Ibiza residency experience, focusing on stripped-back grooves designed for contemplative listening during transitional times of day.

Untold's mix demonstrates a distinctly British approach to techno that subverts genre conventions. The tracklist balances minimal, industrial, and electro elements with an emphasis on unconventional sound design, texture, and percussion. Artists like Blawan, Sleeparchive, and Robag Wruhme represent a cohort of techno producers who respect tradition while deliberately bending rules. The editorial framing emphasizes Dunning's refusal to use templates, resulting in a thoughtful, evolving mix that prioritizes innovation over dancefloor immediacy.

Eli Verveine suspends time between house and techno, drawing from 15 years of vinyl without dating the tracks. Strong microhouse influences (Akufen, Move D) paired with minimal techno fundamentals. The mix emphasizes texture and atmosphere over energy, with dub techno elements creating spatial depth. Pure form DJing rooted in Zurich's club culture.

Apparat curates a deeply personal journey emphasizing melody and atmosphere over rhythm. The mix blends glitchy IDM foundations with ambient soundscapes and subtle techno elements, reflecting his evolution from experimental producer to Berlin's preeminent crossover electronic artist. Selections range from spaced-out synthesizers to delicate vocal moments, creating an introverted, emotionally resonant listening experience.

Mix showcases Vessel's maximalist eclecticism across experimental electronic forms. Heavy emphasis on processed percussion, field recordings, abstract ambient passages, and industrial sound design. Strong connections to UK dub/garage traditions via Young Echo Collective affiliations (Jabu, Kahn, Cut Hands). Incorporates processed vocals, orchestral fragments, and musique concrète techniques. More art-school than dance-floor despite some UK garage touchstones.

DJ Nature's signature style blends roughed-up house with disco samples and dub techniques. The 105-minute mix showcases his lo-fi, sample-heavy approach developed during his 2010 comeback, rooted in his original 'ruff disco' era from early 90s NYC.

Gaslamp Killer's style is defined by genre-agnostic curation and raw sampling aesthetics. His DJ approach merges hip-hop foundations with bass music, jazz, and rock elements in uncompromising, high-energy sets designed to provoke rather than please.

Robert Hood's mix emphasizes his signature minimal techno aesthetic with Detroit roots, balanced between introspective, emotionally resonant production and functional dancefloor energy. Heavy representation from his own catalog (unreleased tracks, M-Plant releases) and Floorplan alias, plus collaborators from Detroit's underground community (DJ Bone, Ben Sims, James Ruskin). The editorial framing highlights 'big, broken-hearted beauty' and socio-political depth—techno with substance rather than pure minimalism.

Joy Orbison's signature style emphasizes restraint and warmth over spectacle. The mix reflects his evolution from his seminal 'Hyph Mngo' era toward a more mature, patient approach to production and DJing. The combination of warm techno with R&B-inflected house elements creates a sophisticated, easy-going aesthetic that rewards close listening.

A Sagittariun's mix explicitly moves through four discrete sonic levels, anchored by touchstones of 90s IDM/techno alongside krautrock and dub. The tracklist spans Biosphere and Eno's ambient minimalism, Detroit techno via Drexciya and Robert Hood, experimental dub via Killing Joke and Dub Syndicate, and krautrock via Can—unified by a celestial, introspective aesthetic. The Bristol producer's own 'Wind Tunnel' serves as a bridging moment between levels.

Mix reflects Pangaea's diverse production palette across garage swing, experimental elements, and techno territory. Curated selection from Hessle Audio peers and contemporary UK producers. Recorded at home; characterized by careful selection and measured pacing rather than peak-time energy.

Danny Daze's sound is rooted in slower, '80s-influenced house with strong electro DNA. The mix showcases classic electro (Anthony Rother, Dexter, Legowelt) blended with contemporary tech house and minimal techno. Tracklist emphasizes instrumental, sample-heavy production with Daze's own edits and remixes integrated throughout. Miami rave heritage and K-addled aesthetic referenced in editorial context.

Michael Mayer's work embodies the Kompakt aesthetic - a refined blend of ambient, kosmische, and minimal techno with strong emotional resonance. The podcast follows his established narrative-focused approach from the Immer series, emphasizing emotional depth over peak-time energy.

Mix showcases the 'hardcore continuum' - the lineage connecting grime, dubstep, and contemporary experimental UK club music. Heavy emphasis on next-generation producers (Wen, Beneath, Visionist) alongside established Keysound roster. The music spans garage-influenced rhythms, glitchy production, and bass-driven experimentation rather than straightforward genre categorization.

Phase (Ashley Burchett) delivers undiluted, high-energy techno with industrial edges and minimal aesthetics. The mix showcases his signature fast-paced rolling sound and features cutting-edge contemporary techno producers. Recorded directly from vinyl and CD in studio without DJ software, emphasizing raw, unfiltered sound design.

Apollonia's mix exemplifies their stated vision of 'white soul and black music' through house music with emphasis on groove and timelessness. The collective's DC-10 residency and end-of-season timing inform the warm, peak-time dancefloor orientation.

October (Jules Smith) mix showcasing TANSTAAFL imprint aesthetic with emphasis on deep house and minimal techno. Features New York/New Jersey deep house practitioners and Berlin-based collaborators. Editorial describes as 'one of the better house and techno mixes of 2012' with 'impossibly smoky aesthetic.' Predominantly instrumental with emphasis on hypnotic, minimal grooves rather than bass music despite Bristol origins.

Art Department are known for slow, bass-focused house with a darker, more minimal aesthetic. This RA podcast specifically highlights their 'darkened underbelly,' emphasizing the introspective and shadowy side of their sound rather than their more anthem-driven tracks.

Matthew Dekay's classical training informs the melodic sophistication of this All Day I Dream showcase. The mix exemplifies the imprint's aesthetic of gorgeous, emotional house music designed for intimate outdoor spaces rather than peak-time dancefloors.

Ron Morelli's L.I.E.S. label aesthetic dominates: heavily experimental house and techno with prominent noise/drone elements, deliberate lo-fi degradation, and unconventional structure (20-minute noise intro). This is underground club music filtered through an avant-garde, deliberately difficult sensibility rather than straightforward dance music.

Mix emphasizes melody and emotional depth within house frameworks. Strong presence of Innervisions-adjacent sound with disco-house influences. Levon Vincent, Young Marco, and Robag Wruhme tracks anchor the minimal-house aesthetic, while Herbert and Ian Pooley remixes add melodic sophistication. The inclusion of The Cheapers and Gerry Diver suggests eclectic song-based curation rather than pure dancefloor functionality.

Answer Code Request (Patrick Gräser) represents contemporary Berlin techno with a distinctive approach informed by '90s breakbeat and minimal aesthetics. The mix reflects his residency at Berghain and demonstrates the multifarious, broken-beat-oriented approach compared to Shed's work.

Mix showcases Jerome Sydenham's evolution from NYC deep house pioneer (via Ibadan Records) to Berlin-based techno artist. Heavy presence of his own edits and remixes demonstrates his editorial sensibility. Tracks feature collaborators central to NY house scene (Kerri Chandler, Dennis Ferrer) alongside Berlin techno figures (Jonas Kopp, Surgeon). Atmosphere balances soulful, atmospheric house elements with driving techno foundations.

Subb-an's style centers on groove-oriented house music tailored for club settings, influenced by DC10's back-to-basics aesthetic. His work spans tech house and deep house through labels like Leftroom and Immigrant, characterized by sophisticated, hypnotic club grooves rather than experimental or avant-garde production.

Zenker Brothers are rooted in Munich's club culture with deep connections to Harry Klein and Rote Sonne. Their sound centers on techno with minimal and Berlin influences, delivered as a live performance featuring entirely new unreleased tracks.

This mix is anchored in experimental and industrial techno with strong drone and noise elements. Artists like Sunn 0))), Vatican Shadow, Raime, and Demdike Stare define a dark, challenging aesthetic. The inclusion of ambient techno (Pan Sonic, Emptyset) and experimental producers (Haxan Cloak, Shackleton) creates an immersive, non-linear narrative. Kerridge's own productions function as connective tissue throughout, binding together a deliberately leftfield and unconventional mix. The tracklist reflects Berlin's experimental electronic scene and a taste for difficult, deconstructed soundscapes rather than conventional club music.

PillowTalk's signature blend of classic soul, disco, and boogie with contemporary house music. Heavy emphasis on vocal-driven tracks with deep emotional resonance. Mix designed for intimate yet club-ready listening, balancing timeless soul recordings with modern remixes and original compositions.

Mix showcases Truss/MPIA3's exploration of extreme, minimalist techno with heavy acid lines and broken beats. Strong UK techno genealogy with tracks from Perc Trax, Ventress, and Avian labels. Tracklist emphasizes functional but intriguing production with 303s, distorted elements, and rave/hardcore undertones.

Ital Tek's signature sound layers footwork's rhythmic template with dubstep's sub-focus and IDM-influenced melodic processing. The 'dual tempo' effect (80 BPM head-nod vs 160 BPM hop) is central to his approach. Mix balances his own prolific catalog with complementary releases from Om Unit, ASC, Reso, and others on labels like Civil Music, White, and Planet Mu.

Dense & Pika's sound centers on warehouse techno with house influences, built on live 909 programming and sampled elements. The mix is described as a full-throttled 31-track set designed for the dancefloor.

Mix reflects the DRM album's bold departure from straight 4/4 club techno into more exploratory, abstract territory. Emphasis on the 'beautifully strange' aesthetic that moves beyond peak-time dancefloor energy.

Jam City's style synthesizes Chicago house, London grime, and Detroit techno into distinctive hybrid productions. The mix emphasizes disparate club sounds with strong bass orientation and R&B influence, reflecting his Night Slugs label ethos of innovation and cross-disciplinary inspiration.

Mix emphasizes the experimental margins of techno rather than peak-time energy. Heavy use of ambient passages, industrial textures, and dub-informed processing. The tracklist features extensive Dadub material alongside complementary artists (Rapoon, Ben Frost, Retina.It, etc.) that prioritize atmospherics and sound design over rhythmic drive. Editorial context confirms this is intentionally pushing beyond conventional techno boundaries.

John Swing's style is characterized by dirty, funky house with a distinctly analog and vintage aesthetic. The mix was recorded on vinyl using turntables and mixer at home, reflecting his label Relative's vinyl-only, live-recorded with analog gear philosophy. His sound combines rawness and funk with warm production elements like deep basslines and sassy vocal samples, all filtered through the warm, dusty character of vinyl records.

Mix heavily features Exit Records catalog with emphasis on advanced, experimental drum and bass. D-Bridge explores electronic elements and 4/4 structures alongside core DnB. Strong presence of Loxy & Resound, Skeptical, and other Exit roster artists pushing genre boundaries.

Solomun's RA.360 mix blends accessible house music with pop sensibilities, reflecting his role as a major figure in the contemporary European house scene and his Ibiza residencies. The mix sits at the intersection of refined tech house and deeper house grooves, designed for peak-time dancefloor impact.

Pearson Sound's mix reflects his post-dubstep innovator status with influences spanning Baltimore club to grime. The editorial notes it's 'a shade or two darker' than expected, suggesting a more introspective, moody approach within the UK bass/dubstep realm while maintaining his signature experimental approach.

Kyle Hall's mix emphasizes raw drum machine textures and bit-crushed synths with deliberate tape distortion. Strong Detroit techno influence through collaborations and mentorship from Rick Wilhite, Mike Huckaby, and Omar-S. The selection favors functional, groove-oriented minimal techno over melodic or trance elements.

Mix showcases Marcellis's diverse 30+ year DJ history across punk, reggae, hip-hop and acid house. Structured narrative arc from gentle house opener through techno and psychedelia to contemplative finale. European house sensibility with experimental psychotronic elements.

Mix demonstrates the blur between techno and house that characterizes Skudge's approach. Editorial mentions the journey from tested foundations through euphoric moments into darker territory, with unexpected instrumentation (trumpet). Studio-based mix from an active label collective.

FunkinEven's signature style blends weird, textured house with acid and techno elements. The mix emphasizes unreleased material and showcases a transatlantic underground sound with collaborators like Kyle Hall and Delroy Edwards. Strong emphasis on unconventional, lo-fi aesthetic within club music framework.

Roberts' style is explicitly described as reductive when labeled simply 'house'—his work blends soaring dance floor elements with velvety experimental cuts. This mix leans toward the shadowy, left-of-centre production aesthetic rather than his typical classic house DJ approach, suggesting ambient and experimental house with unconventional beatwork.

Mix functions as a companion piece to Holden's second album 'The Inheritors,' drawing heavily from 1970s Krautrock pioneers and contemporary experimental electronic artists. Holden's sensibility bridges glitch-era IDM with ambient and industrial textures. Notable inclusions of avant-garde figures (Pharaoh Sanders, Craig Leon, Martial Canterel) alongside Border Community affiliates reflects the label's ethos of melodically-informed, textural electronic music.

Mix showcases Luke Hess's signature blend of dub techno's atmospheric qualities with minimal techno's rhythmic precision. Strong Detroit lineage evident through tracks by Underground Resistance, Octave One, Robert Hood, and Mike Huckaby. Emphasis on analog warmth paired with digital production techniques. Peak-time-adjacent but designed for deeper listening contexts.

A deliberately contrasting portrait of Sven Väth's private sonic palette—the antithesis of his peak-time Cocoon maximalism. Emphasizes textural depth, silence, and emotional nuance over dancefloor functionality. Features collaborators and friends (Roman Flügel, DJ Koze) alongside touchstone figures (Brian Eno, Dead Can Dance) who shaped his broader musical worldview. The mix unfolds as a slow-burn meditation rather than a narrative arc, ideal for late-night home listening or solitary reflection.

Mix blends reed-based live jazz improvisation with electronic and dance music foundations. Strong emphasis on atmospheric, experimental approach rather than peak-time club energy. Archie Pelago's use of saxophones/reeds creates unique fusion of formal music training with underground electronic aesthetics.

Black Coffee's signature style blends soulful house with African percussion and melody. The mix showcases contemporary South African house music with emphasis on vocals and emotional depth rather than pure techno minimalism.

Akkord (Synkro & Indigo) creates music at the intersection of techno, bass music, and IDM with emphasis on intricate rhythmic structures and imposing low-end. The mix showcases influential tracks plus recent productions, recorded live in Ableton.

Helena Hauff's mix deliberately combines EBM, new beat, and acid sounds as stated in her own description. The tracklist heavily features industrial and electro pioneers (Front 242, The Neon Judgement, DMX Krew) alongside contemporary acid and electro producers. Recorded at home on vinyl, reflecting the tactile, experimental ethos of the Golden Pudel club scene in Hamburg.

Outboxx's signature sound emphasizes melody, harmony and songwriting within house music, eschewing lo-fi trends in favor of warm, sunshine-drenched grooves with soulful vocals and funk influences. The mix balances uplifting daytime energy with deeper, spacious moments.

Blondes curate a sophisticated techno mix emphasizing dense, evolving soundscapes and acid-tinged minimalism. The selection reflects their own compositional philosophy of extended arrangements with subtle, deliberate progression. Artists like Efdemin, Terrence Dixon, and Laurel Halo represent thoughtful, texture-driven approaches to dance music.

Oneman's signature style blends UK garage foundations with dubstep, grime and house influences. The mix showcases his reputation as a fearless vinyl DJ with expert blending across tempos and styles. Strong presence of UK club producers (Zed Bias, Boddika, MJ Cole, Redinho) alongside contemporary artists. The selection emphasizes functional dancefloor music across multiple genres rather than a single aesthetic.

Dennis Ferrer is known for bridging underground credibility with broader appeal through well-crafted house music that balances deep basslines with memorable hooks. The mix reflects his signature style of contemporary house rooted in classic traditions.

Anton Zap's style transcends traditional club categories—the editorial notes 'hardly any house or techno in sight,' suggesting this mix prioritizes ambient, experimental, and atmospheric sounds over conventional dance structures. His approach is characterized by poetic mood and obscure sourcing rather than recognizable genre markers.

Juju & Jordash's music transcends traditional genre boundaries through live synth jamming. Their fusion of house/techno foundations with jazz and post-rock sensibilities, combined with the improvisational nature of this podcast recording, positions them in experimental electronic territory with strong Kosmische and ambient techno elements.

Joey Anderson's style is characterized by unconventional drum placement (avoiding snares/claps on 2 and 4), bittersweet melodic elements, and an unresolved tension. The tracklist showcases Detroit techno pioneers (Roy Davis Jr., Omar S, D'Marc Cantu) alongside contemporary deep/minimal practitioners, reflecting the aesthetic of the New York/New Jersey underground house and techno network.

A deliberately fragmented mix that interrogates dance music through experimental composition. Features canonical electroacoustic works (Xenakis, Amacher, Behrman) punctuated by one conventional house track, embodying Fell's ambivalent relationship with club culture. Recorded on laptop as a curated listening experience rather than DJ performance.

Spencer Parker explicitly states his core interests are house, disco, and techno, which he blends dynamically depending on context. The mix was recorded with vinyl and digital equipment, reflecting a modern DJ's hybrid approach. The editorial emphasizes his multifaceted creative output and the colorful, rugged palette of his sound.

Trentemøller's curation reflects his evolution away from dance music toward raw, guitar-based experimental sounds. Artists like Soft Moon, A Place To Bury Strangers, and Beak represent contemporary post-punk and experimental noise rather than electronic dance traditions. The mix showcases influences from his cited inspirations (Suicide, The Raveonettes) and aligns with his recent shift toward fully-realized alternative music with instrumental textures.

FCL represent a committed, fundamentalist approach to house music with emphasis on timeless, dancefloor-tested tracks rather than trend-chasing. Strong connection to We Play House label as platform for quality 12-inch releases.

Daniel Avery's signature sound blends techno, electro, and ambient elements with a distinctly noisy, machine-centric aesthetic. The 'machine funk' descriptor and album title 'Drone Logic' indicate a focus on hypnotic, modular sounds and abstract textures rather than traditional club beats. His background in indie/guitar music informs an unconventional approach to dance music production.

Machinedrum's mix blends footwork's rapid polyrhythms with jungle's emotive intensity and IDM's textural sophistication. The tracklist showcases a thriving community of UK and international producers reimagining fast-tempo bass music with melodic and atmospheric elements. Concept-driven listening experience reflecting the 'Vapor City' album theme.

Traumprinz delivers tough, shadowy house music with minimal and dub techno influences. The mix is based largely on unreleased tracks from the forthcoming Mothercave album, recorded on tape at Sender Geibel radio station. His work through Giegling label exemplifies a contemporary Berlin-influenced, vinyl-only approach to dance music that prioritizes restraint and hypnotic repetition over flashiness.

Fis produces music deliberately outside conventional grid-based production, drawing inspiration from bodily meditation rather than traditional beat structures. The mix emphasizes strange, tar-like synthesis and unconventional rhythmic approaches rather than dancefloor functionality.
Luke Slater's RA podcast captures a gradual journey from house foundations (L.B. Dub Corp material) to pure techno (P.A.S. material), unified by meticulous sound design and released on Ostgut Ton

Deepchord deliberately deviating from his signature dub techno template into more experimental territory. The mix emphasizes abstract sound design over traditional song structures, reflecting Modell's recent shift away from beats toward textural exploration.

Mix showcases Night Slugs label's signature sound: inventive percussive breakdowns blending juke, grime, and UK garage sensibilities. Bok Bok demonstrates mastery of drum programming and rhythmic arrangement across emerging and established producers from the Night Slugs/Fade To Mind network. The 'Club Constructions' series influence is evident throughout—lo-fi textures paired with intricate, thrill-seeking grooves.

Conforce represents Boris Bunnik's atmospheric techno project, characterized by a broad palette ranging from fragile ambient to heavy dancefloor techno, with emphasis on spatial production and sound design over traditional arrangements.

Om Unit's mix showcases the intersection of drum and bass, dubstep, and footwork—working primarily at 160 BPM. The tracklist features key figures in the contemporary bass music scene (J Kenzo, Sam Binga, Machinedrum, Tessela) and spans Planet Mu, Metalheadz, and Civil Music—labels foundational to this ecosystem. The editorial context emphasizes Om Unit's trajectory from 2Tall (hip-hop) through dubstep into rhythm-focused bass music, with footwork's impact on his faster-tempo work being central to his artistic vision.

Mix transitions from ambient, meditative deep house in opening tracks (emphasizing melody and atmosphere from Leif's album Dinas Oleu) into deeper, more textured minimalist techno in second half. Strong emphasis on production quality, atmospheric depth, and rhythmic subtlety rather than dancefloor intensity.

Parasole is characterized as a 'first-class selector and producer of tough techno' with roots in New York's techno scene. The mix is described as 'characteristically fierce' with a 'New York-centric twist.' His work through The Corner label and earlier Deconstruct output positions him in the minimal/Detroit techno spectrum rather than melodic or ambient variants.

Midland's mix showcases his signature approach: brooding, introspective house and techno that bridges Ibiza accessibility with Panorama Bar credibility. The tracklist moves through emotive, dub-influenced deep house (Soulphiction, Bruce, Kyle Hall) and atmospheric ambient passages (Tangerine Dream, Four Tet) with disciplined pacing—a deliberate counterpoint to typical New Year euphoria. Dark, sophisticated, and introspective throughout.

Mix showcases Haslam's sophisticated approach to modern techno, blending acid elements with experimental and leftfield club music. Strong presence of L.I.E.S. and Kompakt releases reflects his artistic philosophy. Tracklist emphasizes cerebral, spacious soundscapes over peak-time dancefloor energy.

Mix spans 1970s funk/soul foundations (Patrice Rushen, Lowrell references) through Detroit and Berlin techno minimalism (Kraftwerk, Stingray, Shakir), unified by Rebelle's signature quality of era-blending selectivity. The tracklist shows deliberate pacing: soulful grooves establishing warmth early, building through house and garage textures (Lady Blacktronika, Cooly G), culminating in abstract techno (Martyn, FaltyDL). Editorial context emphasizes this is curated for intimate listening rather than peak-time urgency.

Sunil Sharpe's mix draws heavily from classic Detroit and Berlin techno lineages with contemporary experimental edges. Tracks span foundational references (Speedy J, Robert Owens, DJ Deeon) through cutting-edge underground producers, reflecting his reputation for intense, technically sophisticated selections. The editorial context emphasizes his exceptional DJ skills, varied tastes, and role as Dublin's leading techno advocate, positioning this as a definitive showcase of his uncompromising aesthetic.

Sadar Bahar represents the deep Chicago house tradition rooted in Ron Hardy's influence at the Music Box. His approach emphasizes rare vinyl selections, soulful house foundations, and funk influences, creating sets that prioritize depth and distinctiveness over trend-following.

Bill Converse represents the contemporary outsider electronic artist making music independent of trends, influenced by labels like Mood Hut and L.I.E.S. The mix compiles dusty, unconventional samples and sounds that prioritize artistic vision over dancefloor utility, reflecting his production philosophy while showcasing his skills as a DJ.

Mix heavily rooted in breakcore's fractured, polyrhythmic aesthetic, but distinguished by strong melodic sensibilities and serious compositional approach. Features collaborations with related artists in the underground breakcore/experimental scene (Igorrr, Venetian Snares). Blends breakcore's core strategy of genre-mashup with jazz, funk, and folk influences. Contemporary underground sound drawing from 1990s breakcore's roots while emphasizing sophistication over shock value.

DJ Deeon is a pioneer of ghetto house, a distinctly Chicago subgenre characterized by fast breakbeats, explicit themes, and customized drum machine production that emerged from South Side party culture.

Soichi Terada's style blends deep house foundations with jungle elements and ambient textures, characterized by sophisticated melodic arrangement and warm, organic sound design rooted in 1990s Far East Recording output.

A carefully curated mix showcasing modern bass music from Hard Wax's sphere of influence, blending grime, dubstep, dub and techno with emphasis on unreleased cuts and dubplates. Hops presents a refined, sub-focused sound that bridges UK bass traditions with contemporary club sensibilities.

Mix exemplifies the Robert Hood school of minimalism where groove and incremental arrangement changes drive the music. Suckut's curation emphasizes powerful, precisely-tooled club tracks built from fundamental elements—kick drums, hi-hats, sparse synths. Labels like Rekids, Chain Reaction, and Running Back confirm deep roots in Detroit and Berlin minimalist techno traditions.

Mix emphasizes restraint and emotional depth over dancefloor utility. Composed of unreleased productions that showcase Legov's enigmatic approach to club music—soothing and meditative rather than peak-time driven.

Keita Sano is described as a 'maddest and most interesting' producer operating between house and techno. The mix emphasizes unreleased tracks with prominent basslines and acid elements ('Color Acid' title), delivered as slamming peak-time material. Editorial context highlights his stylistic variety and experimental approach, though this particular mix focuses on harder, driving house rather than his more ambient/broken beat work.

Offermann is a modern deep house specialist known for functional yet vibrant production. This mix emphasizes groove as a foundation for delicate, colourful layers—distinctly warm but with ethereal, spacious qualities that separate it from standard club deep house.

Mix draws from multiple African countries and decades, featuring funk, highlife, synth-pop, praise music, and electronic experimentalism sourced from cassette markets. The curation reflects Brian Shimkovitz's ethnomusicological expertise and passion for preserving and reviving lesser-known African artists.
Del Garda's style emphasizes technical mixing of diverse sources with emphasis on deep crate-digging and warm, infectious energy on the dancefloor

Mix showcases Kangding Ray's producer-DJ approach, blending strict techno with ambient passages and experimental sound design. Tracks from Raster-Noton, Tresor, Northern Electronics, and other intellectually-oriented labels confirm the cerebral, non-commercial aesthetic. Steve Reich and Laurie Spiegel inclusions underscore the classical/compositional underpinning.

Sonja Moonear represents the minimal scene at its highest level—a DJ known for impeccable taste rather than prolific production. This mix showcases her signature blend of stripped-back techno and house music with rhythmic complexity and dancefloor intelligence.

The Range creates 'emotional electronica' that blends club music elements with deeply personal, vulnerable songwriting. The mix features unreleased material alongside catalog tracks, emphasizing Hinton's distinctive approach of drawing sentiment from sampled voices and sincere melodic sensibilities rather than traditional dance music conventions.

Mix demonstrates classic warm-up approach learned from Theo Parrish and François K at Plastic People. Smooth, unfussy ebb and flow with deep house and techno selections that gradually build energy without rushing. Strong editorial context confirms warm-up aesthetic over peak-time intensity.

Mix showcases Tessela's approach to bridging UK bass heritage with forward-thinking techno. Tracklist emphasizes producers from the post-2008 UK scene who elevated dubstep's collapse into durable, architecturally sophisticated club music. Combines rave-influenced percussion with minimal aesthetics and industrial textures.

Boys Noize showcases his evolution from electro house toward harder, darker territories—blending EBM touchstones (DAF, Nitzer Ebb), classic industrial (Esplendor Geometrico, Prostitutes), and contemporary techno (Tzusing, Sleeparchive). The mix emphasizes martial rhythms and unconventional arrangements rather than dancefloor accessibility, capturing the 'strange side' referenced in the blurb.

Dr. Rubinstein (Marina Rubinstein) constructs a gripping arc through obscure, emotionally charged techno records. The tracklist features labels like Ilian Tape, Killekill, and Forum—all associated with contemporary Berlin's harder, more experimental techno aesthetic. Strong emphasis on acid elements (303003, E.R.P.), industrial textures (Umwelt, Vril), and mind-bending production. This is sophisticated, record-collector techno with visual and psychedelic qualities rather than straightforward dancefloor fare.

Karenn specializes in raw, improvised live techno with UK dance music roots. The set emphasizes stripped-down sound design with industrial elements, strong emphasis on dynamic control and emotional range over extended performances.

Red Greg specializes in obscure, independently-released disco, boogie and soul records. While 'Boogie' is not in the provided vocabulary, it is central to his style and has been included. The mix emphasizes rare, deep cuts selected for dancefloor functionality rather than mainstream appeal.

Mix showcases seminal grime and garage tracks from the 2000s UK underground, curated to highlight the raw, stripped-back aesthetic that influenced Raime's exploration of UK club music through an experimental lens. Heavy emphasis on rhythm sections with characteristic off-beat hi-hats and sub-bass.

Lazare Hoche specializes in a lean, no-frills house sound with emphasis on repetitive loops and subtle emotional depth rather than maximalist production. The mix features smooth transitions and a steady, shuffling rhythm foundation characteristic of contemporary European house music.

MCDE and Jeremy Underground represent a modern digger DJ aesthetic blending house foundations with obsessively curated soul, funk, disco and boogie records from around the world. Nine-hour live residency set at Robert Johnson emphasizes technique, vinyl culture, and a warm, introspective approach to extended DJ sets.
Mix recorded on vinyl with Technics turntables, reflects Baggs' eclectic approach blending house, techno, electro and garage influences from his Berlin clubbing background

Mix draws from classic American house and techno traditions (Chicago, Detroit, New York) with emphasis on joyful, energetic dancefloor music. Includes key tracks from Underground Resistance, Moodymann, and Floorplan representing Detroit's legacy, alongside contemporary house interpreters. The 'celebratory' framing and track selection emphasizes warmth and uplift over experimentalism.

Tijana T's mix centers on stripped-down, energetic techno with deep roots in Detroit and minimal house traditions. The tracklist features essential figures in underground techno (Kowton, Pearson Sound, Vakula, Floorplan) alongside classic house and techno pioneers (Robert Armani, Heller & Farley Project). The editorial context emphasizes the 'ravey, exhilarating, warehouse techno' character tied to Belgrade's resilient late-90s club scene, creating an atmosphere that is both historically grounded and intensely present-focused.

Mix Mup (Lorenz Lindner) specializes in boundary-pushing electronic music that defies strict categorization. The aesthetic blends house and techno fundamentals with mechanical funk textures, sloppy-but-intentional production techniques, and avant-garde sensibilities. Frequent UR-inspired synth work and lumbering basslines anchor deliberately chaotic arrangements.

Mix centered on Studio Barnhus aesthetic: house music foundation with techno and microhouse flourishes. Strong emphasis on Swedish and Northern European producers. Tracks blend dub-inflected grooves with subtle samples and jazzy textures. Pacing designed for sustained dance-floor momentum through early morning hours.

The Exaltics (Robert Witschakowski) is positioned as a modern electro purist working in the Drexciya lineage. Solar One Music releases and collaborations with Gerald Donald (Heinrich Mueller/Drexciya) confirm deep roots in Detroit techno and classic electro aesthetics. The mix prioritizes instrumental, sample-driven electro with a focus on quality vinyl releases from the contemporary scene.

James Ruskin RA podcast mix showcasing contemporary UK techno with emphasis on fractured rhythms and experimental production. Tracklist heavily features Blueprint label artists and collaborators (Lakker, Surgeon, Oliver Ho, Autechre, Regis). Mix demonstrates evolution from mid-90s UK techno roots while incorporating IDM and industrial influences. Editorial context emphasizes artistic finesse and the label's 20+ year legacy.

DJ Spider's signature style combines heavy, grinding industrial techno with experimental sound design and hypnotic rhythms. The mix features predominantly unreleased material and catalog cuts that showcase his distinctive approach to dark, bass-driven production with unusual textural elements (dragging pickaxes, car alarms, etc.). Strong emphasis on percussive interplay and groove despite the stark, dystopian sonic palette.

rRoxymore's sound defies conservative club music norms through experimental production and psychedelic atmospherics applied to house and techno foundations. The mix reflects her multidisciplinary background (electronic percussion, acousmatic composition, rare groove) creating a contemporary take that balances club functionality with experimental sound design.

Zaltan's mix reflects his label philosophy—no unified sound, but unified taste. Drawing from Paris flea markets and global funk/soul with contemporary electronic sensibilities. Best described as a carefully curated home-listening experience rather than a club-focused set.

Henrik Bergqvist draws from classic house, tech house, UK bleep, and 90s techno. The RA podcast emphasizes his role in Stockholm's The Office warehouse parties and his ability to blend tracks old and new with expert pacing. This mix represents his more upbeat, accessible side rather than darker material.

Beautiful Swimmers exemplify the fringe dance music circuit approach—mixing UK garage and house with jungle, Baltimore club, and post-punk influences. Their selections emphasize fun and danceability over genre purity, reflecting their work as serious record collectors and label operators.

Loxy's mix showcases the depth and evolution of drum and bass, balancing jungle influences with modern experimentation. His deliberate mixing style and curation of landmark releases reflect his position as a genre architect rather than trend-chaser.

Henning Severud's Telephones project exemplifies modern Balearic house with deep house sensibilities, characterized by accessible yet sophisticated production that bridges classic Balearic pioneers like Jose Padilla with contemporary electronic aesthetics

Ziúr's mix exemplifies experimental club music with tough, clashing sounds that prioritize artistic expression over dancefloor smoothness. The eclecticism is strategic—pairing mainstream pop (Nicki Minaj, Rihanna) with avant-garde selections (Amnesia Scanner, Brian Eno) and jazz (Duke Ellington), creating conceptual tension rather than mere variety. The mix reflects Berlin's radical club culture and Ziúr's commitment to both sonic innovation and progressive values.

Mix spans techno, electro, and house with emphasis on refined, minimal approaches. Tresor resident with strong Detroit techno and ghettotech elements. Editorial context emphasizes depth, mystery, and years of artistic refinement rather than flashy showmanship.

Mix blends ambient, krautrock-influenced synth works (Klaus Schulze, Dieter Schütz) with sparse, experimental house and downtempo material. Reflects RVDS's philosophy of genre-transcendence rooted in house but exploring adjacent territories. Strong emphasis on texture and atmosphere over dancefloor functionality.

John FM represents the new generation of Detroit producers blending techno foundations with experimental electroacoustic sensibilities and soulful vocals. His work extends beyond dancefloor functionality into deeply personal, introspective territory.

Mix emphasizes Volvox's eclectic, personality-filled approach to acid and electro techno, reflecting her Bossa Nova Civic Club aesthetic rather than her harder warehouse sound. Strong focus on oddball, industrial-tinged tracks with character.

AYBEE's mix opens with indigenous Tewa music and blends deep jazz influences (Jaco Pastorius, Billy Cobham, Roy Ayers) with contemporary experimental house/techno. The sound sits between functional dancefloor techno and exploratory, genre-bending production—characterized by irregular rhythms and soul-driven textures rather than straight 4/4 beats. Strong Miles Davis/spiritual jazz lineage evident throughout.

Black Motion exemplifies the Afro House sound emerging from South Africa, characterized by positive energy and deep connection to local musical traditions rather than dark, introspective aesthetics common in European house music.

Tuff City Kids deliver a playful, high-energy mix blending their signature melody-rich house and disco with favorite 80s new wave and EBM cuts. The selection emphasizes big, singalong moments and booming drums—designed as a feel-good party mix rather than a deep-listening experience. Strong editorial context confirms their aesthetic: delicate touch, expert pacing, informed by decades of record collecting.

Mark Barrott's mix epitomizes contemporary Balearic philosophy—a curated journey blending world music (Oumou Sangaré), art rock (David Sylvian, Talk Talk), deep electronic (Pépé Bradock), and cinematic soundscapes. The inclusion of spoken word (Maya Angelou, Hokusai) and acoustic elements (Richard Hawley) reinforces the mood of reflective ease. This is not club Balearic but rather the meditative, cosmopolitan variant that prioritizes artistic depth and cultural eclecticism over dancefloor functionality.

Craig Richards' fabric residency defined UK underground house and techno culture from 1999 onwards. Mix emphasizes deep, spacious sounds with emphasis on musical restraint and global influence (particularly South American techno via Villalobos). Three-hour set suggests extended journey through refined club sound.

Mix emphasizes atmospheric, slow-burn techno and ambient passages interspersed with post-punk and experimental electroacoustic elements. Strong focus on non-Western samples and field recordings, particularly Middle Eastern and Brazilian material. Moscoman's curatorial approach bridges contemporary electronic music with world music traditions, creating an introspective rather than peak-time aesthetic.

Varg (Jonas Rönnberg) creates a singular blend of techno with industrial and ambient elements, characteristic of the Northern Electronics label. This 45-minute live set showcases his Nordic Flora series with a narrative arc from unsettling ambience through peak energy to broken beat resolution, emphasizing the melancholic yet driving character of his work.

A deeply cohesive mix rooted in industrial, EBM and experimental techno. The tracklist reveals strong influences from Bunker Records and leftfield electronic outlets, with artists like Esplendor Geometrico, Clock DVA, and Kirk Degiorgio establishing a lineage of harsh, mechanical sounds. The editorial context emphasizes Mauer's isolation-shaped taste and her residence at St. Petersburg's Stackenschneider, positioning this as distinctly Eastern European underground club music with post-punk and industrial DNA.

Egyptrixx's mix blends fractured club music aesthetics with experimental sound design. The tracklist features abstract producers (Taraval, Nisennenmondai, Untold) mixed with footwork and rave-adjacent tracks (DJ Deeon, Pressa), creating a deliberately warped club experience that prioritizes structural and sonic experimentation over conventional dancefloor conventions. The editorial context emphasizes Psutka's evolution from beat-driven Night Slugs material toward increasingly ambient and textural work, sometimes dropping beats entirely—reflected in tracks like Ceramic TL's ethereal piece and the contemplative elements throughout.

Vladimir Ivkovic's style is characterized by an 'abrasive, always intriguing' approach. His Belgrade roots and 12-year residency at Düsseldorf's Salon Des Amateurs position him within the experimental techno ecosystem. The 'sublime to sludgy' descriptor suggests dynamic range from ethereal to harsh textures, typical of contemporary industrial and experimental techno practitioners.
Mix spans electronic pioneers (Hosono, Sakamoto), European concrète, American avant-garde, and Chicago house - unified by futurist sensibility rather than single genre

Kingdom's mix exemplifies the cross-genre approach pioneered by Fade To Mind, weaving ballroom, grime, Jersey club, and R&B with house and pop sensibilities. The 'sweat and tears' aesthetic reflects both the physical club experience and emotional depth of contemporary experimental club music.

Nathan Fake's mix showcases IDM and ambient techno foundations with strong experimental and electroacoustic elements. The tracklist features key figures in intelligent electronic music (Aphex Twin, Orbital, Minor Science) alongside more obscure producers, reflecting Fake's described shift toward 'sharper and more urgent' sounds while maintaining his trademark melodic sensibility. The presence of Vatican Shadow indicates embrace of darker, noisier textures. This is contemporary UK experimental electronic music with introspective depth rather than dancefloor energy.

Capriati is characterized by direct, high-impact techno with tight control; strong connections to Berlin Techno scene (Beyer/Drumcode) and Ibiza club culture; known for commanding main room sets with enthusiastic crowds

KABLAM's set prioritizes emotional impact over genre coherence, mixing experimental club tracks, glitchy production, industrial sounds, and heavily edited/recontextualized samples. The tracklist reveals an aesthetic of controlled chaos: works by Amnesia Scanner, M.E.S.H., and Swan Meat sit alongside bootleg edits of mainstream tracks (Rihanna, Evanescence), reflecting her DIY editing practice. The mix embodies the Janus collective's anti-smooth, affect-driven approach to club music.
Mix focuses on experimental deconstruction of dancehall through dub techniques and collage production. Emphasizes B-sides and dubplate culture rather than mainstream dancehall.

This mix centers on obscure, weird pop and disco records from the 1980s, curated by a dedicated record digger in Tokyo. The 'psychic dance' theme emphasizes the surreal, dreamlike quality of the selections rather than peak-time energy. While house elements are present, the primary focus is on rare disco variants, synth-pop oddities, and funk-inflected pop records that straddle multiple genres. The mix is vinyl-heavy and reflects deep knowledge of Japanese and international digging culture.

Mix emphasizes atmospheric, spacious production with strong dub and ambient techno influences. Artists like Levon Vincent, Moritz Von Oswald, and Donato Dozzy signal deep, minimal aesthetic. The 'moodier' character described in editorial aligns with dub techno and ambient techno elements, while maintaining functional house sensibility for dance floor application.

Svreca curates a deeply introspective techno session emphasizing textural subtlety and emotional depth over dancefloor functionality. The mix showcases the contemporary techno scene's emphasis on incremental innovation and distinctive modern production, with collaborators representing techno's most respected artists. Strong emphasis on atmospheric immersion and loss of temporal awareness.

Mix bridges Willow's primary deep house/tech house output with formative influences in classic hip-hop and trip-hop (Missy Elliott, Portishead). Tracklist shows her taste for minimalist UK house (Joy Orbison, Isolee) and contemporary producers while grounding the mix in soulful, textured production. The blend of club-ready tracks with introspective downtempo elements reflects her stated musical range.

A curated personal selection of pop music spanning decades, sequenced with emotional intelligence rather than DJ technique. Reflects Voigt's softer aesthetic alongside his electronic work, emphasizing melody, arrangement sophistication, and emotional resonance over dancefloor functionality.

Young Marco's style emphasizes melodic, atmospheric house music with strong influences from ambient and experimental electronic music, particularly through his Gaussian Curve project. His DJ approach is characterized by eclectic selection across genres including synth pop, electro, and Dutch rap, with emphasis on reissue culture and thoughtful curation rather than trend-chasing.

Haruka curates a deliberately paced, synth-rich set with strong psychedelic character. The mix features luminaries from Detroit, UK IDM, and Japanese techno scenes (Nobu, Cortex, Craig, Autechre, Air Liquide), emphasizing atmospheric building and emotional depth over dancefloor immediacy. This is considered an exceptional warmup rather than peak-time material.

DJ Koolt represents the minimalist dance music scene of Montevideo, Uruguay. The mix draws from tech house and early techno with a distinctive percussive, groovy aesthetic developed through frequent residency play at Phonotheque. The set moves from pumping peak-time material into more abstract territory.

Mix reflects Flørist's ability to blend classic house/disco with obscure deep cuts and techno, capturing the aesthetic and energy of Vancouver's thriving scene. The tracklist spans '90s deep house, vintage electronic, obscure edits, and contemporary club cuts—emphasizing discovery and spontaneity over a unified narrative.
SIT specializes in mesmerizing minimal loops informed by classical training. The mix emphasizes hypnotic, detail-rich soundscapes suited to extended DJ sets, recorded live with full studio setup.

Mr Mendel's approach transcends traditional genre boundaries by drawing from five decades of global music—African (Fela Kuti), American soul/funk (Nina Simone, James Brown), and Brazilian music—unified through a soulful, dancefloor sensibility. The mix emphasizes edits that preserve soul and dynamics while reimagining the original material.

Saoirse constructs a sophisticated, deliberately challenging mix drawing from Detroit techno (DBX, Octave One, Paul Johnson), Berlin industrial techno (Bjarki, Arca), and experimental electronic music (Shackleton, Arca). The progression favors stripped-down, hypnotic grooves with occasional jarring, experimental textures. Tracks like Arca's 'Piel' (bookending the mix), Shackleton's 'Death Is Not Final', and Bjarki's contributions anchor the mix in contemporary avant-garde techno. This is sophisticated, underground-leaning dance music—not euphoric or melodic, but intellectually demanding and sonically textured.

Mark Broom's signature style emphasizes groove and swing within a techno framework. The tracklist features key Detroit figures (Carl Craig, Robert Hood, Cybotron) and contemporary minimalist techno artists (Tolkachev, Dodd). This is lean, functional techno with rhythmic sophistication rather than experimental abstraction.

Courtesy's mix reflects her stated aesthetic of contrasts: linear beats colliding with broken rhythms, sinister atmospheres with warmth, and scuzzy textures alternating with clean production. The tracklist heavily features artists from experimental and industrial techno circles (Orphx, Neinzer, Special Request, Yen Towers) alongside abstract ambient pieces (Suzanne Ciani), all unified by an urgent, stark sensibility rooted in noise and industrial music. The selection prioritizes records with 'original ways of directing energy on a dance floor' while maintaining a fiercely modern, often avant-garde approach.

Rahaan represents the lineage connecting classic Chicago house and New York disco pioneers to contemporary selectors. His style emphasizes edited funk and soul records played with the warmth and musicality of the original disco era, bridging vintage and modern sensibilities.

Hodge's mix exemplifies Bristol's adventurous approach to techno, fusing heavy bass weight from the dubstep era with breakbeats and industrial textures. The tracklist showcases genre-fluid producers (Pearson Sound, Pangaea, Tessela) who resist straightforward categorization. Despite 'Techno' being the primary genre, the music frequently incorporates drum and bass breakbeats and dub influences, reflecting the city's post-dubstep legacy.

Mix heavily features Steve Bicknell's 6dimensions label releases alongside collaborators Metro Skim and Heartless. The tracklist suggests stripped-back, hypnotic minimal techno with Detroit influences - consistent with Bicknell's 30-year legacy of inventive minimalism and the Lost party's 'music beamed down from another galaxy' aesthetic. Dark, knowledge-driven underground sensibility.

Mix draws heavily from soul, funk and disco records with house sensibilities. Tracklist features classic soul artists (Eddie Holman, Mighty Clouds of Joy, Jaki Whitren) alongside contemporary edits, reflecting CC:DISCO!'s philosophy of joyful, accessible dancefloor music over intellectualism. The editing choices (Psychemagik, Swifft, Super Drama) modernize vintage material for contemporary club play.

Galaxian's mix blends fast, aggressive electro and techno with IDM-influenced glitch and experimental production. Strong Detroit lineage (Underground Resistance, DJ Stingray) mixed with UK experimentalism (Aphex Twin, Oneohtrix Point Never, Squarepusher). The mix's energy is relentless and cerebral rather than euphoric—designed to provoke thought alongside physicality. Titles and editorial context emphasize anti-establishment themes and consciousness-disruption messaging.

Mix emphasizes Griffin James's DJ approach spanning multiple styles rather than his more textured production work. 'Party music for the apocalypse' suggests high-energy club music with surrealist/dystopian framing.

Zadig presents a deliberate inversion of his techno roots, moving away from dance-floor functionality toward contemplative, textural electronic music. The mix features established experimental and ambient techno artists (Monolake, Anthony Child, Photek, Vakula) in a carefully curated 90-minute session emphasizing space, depth, and introspection over rhythm.

Mix reflects Baba Stiltz's signature approach: accessible house/techno foundations with unexpected production choices and emotional nuance. Tracks suggest a warm-up or deeper club session with personality—not peak-time bombast but rather thoughtful, quirky dancefloor music. Strong Stockholm/Scandinavian contingent evident in tracklist (Nystrand von Unge, Little Jinder references). The editorial context emphasizes Stiltz's refusal to follow convention while remaining rooted in house music fundamentals.

Jamie Tiller's approach emphasizes funk, soul, and house with global influences sourced from deep record digging. The mix reflects his philosophy of discovering undervalued records rather than chasing status, suggesting accessible grooves across multiple genres with a focus on feel-good, soulful material rather than niche experimental territory.

Erika draws from Interdimensional Transmissions' 30-year Detroit techno legacy while incorporating harder industrial and electro elements. The mix balances brutalist production with moments of ethereal texture, reflecting her dual mastery of floor-working and experimental aesthetics. Emphasis on contemporary underground producers working in dark, minimalist techno spaces.

Jon K's mix exemplifies a DJ philosophy that prioritizes mood evolution and unconventional track selection over genre purity. The tracklist moves through beatless ambient passages (Luxos, Total Capacity), experimental electronic pieces (EP4, Nine Circles), and sparse dub-influenced cuts (Loefah, Pepe Bradock), creating a journey that starts contemplative and gradually intensifies. The inclusion of beatless tracks and spoken word elements (Leslie Winer, John Bender) alongside club-oriented material demonstrates his signature approach to building momentum through atmosphere rather than consistent rhythm.

Noncompliant (formerly DJ Shiva) delivers searing, punk-inflected techno with industrial undertones. The tracklist features hard-hitting cuts from labels like Tresor, Downwards, and Pinkman—cornerstone underground imprints. The mix emphasizes relentless, driving rhythms and an in-your-face aesthetic rooted in Midwest rave culture and decades of underground practice.

DJ Minx's signature style blends Detroit house fundamentals with soulful samples, disco swagger, and deep techno undercurrents. The mix prioritizes joyful, uplifting energy over darkness—distinctly optimistic house music designed to move bodies and lift spirits.

Phase Fatale's mix draws heavily from 80s industrial, EBM, and post-punk sources, fusing them with contemporary techno aesthetics. The tracklist features artists deeply rooted in industrial and EBM traditions (Haus Arafna, Grauzone, Orphx, Esplendor Geométrico) alongside contemporary industrial techno producers. The overall character is aggressive and textural rather than dancefloor-focused, with emphasis on distortion, metallic tones, and atmospheric darkness.

Mix celebrates Arthur Russell's diverse catalog spanning disco, folk, modern composition, and lullabies. Nick The Record's deep archival knowledge and connection to Russell's music creates a historically grounded, intimate listening experience rather than a traditional club mix.

M.E.S.H.'s mix exemplifies experimental/deconstructed club music with complex layering, unconventional drum programming, and fragmented rhythmic structures. The tracklist features key artists from the Berlin experimental club scene (Tzusing, DJ Richard, Pinch & Mumdance, Hyph11e). Music is characterized by information-dense arrangements, explosive production, and a deliberate rejection of traditional dance music structures while maintaining dancefloor functionality.

Oscar Mulero's signature style: evolved from tough, loop-heavy early 2000s sound toward more sophisticated, heady techno. Strong emphasis on texture and spatial production. Editorial context emphasizes his role in establishing Spain as part of European techno conversation.

Mix centers on spiritual jazz, classic soul, and Brazilian music with emphasis on melody and emotional depth. Reflects Mafalda's identity as a London-based music curator and Melodies International co-founder who bridges jazz club culture with soul records.

Mix emphasizes textural innovation and rhythmic unpredictability within techno framework. Strong influence from early 90s Aphex Twin-style IDM alongside Detroit and vintage electro. Kristensen's approach prioritizes breaking genre conventions while maintaining dancefloor credibility.

Mix showcases Partok's opening-set approach with stripped-back, hypnotic techno that emphasizes texture and atmosphere over dancefloor immediacy. Heavy influence from the Berlin and UK industrial/experimental techno scenes, with key artists like Surgeon, Jeff Mills, and Demdike Stare establishing a dark, introspective mood. The Block's legendary soundsystem paired with the carefully curated tracklist creates an immersive, almost ritualistic listening experience typical of underground techno culture.

The Black Madonna's 600th RA podcast showcases her signature eclecticism, blending Chicago house foundations with post-punk (Talking Heads), new wave (INXS, The B-52s), classic funk and disco alongside contemporary house and techno selections. The mix demonstrates her deep record collection spanning decades while maintaining dancefloor momentum and uplifting energy throughout.

Neel's signature style blends minimal techno frameworks with ambient sensibilities and exceptional sound design clarity. The mix balances pumping club elements with the ethereal, textural qualities established in his earlier ambient work.

Etienne specializes in minimal techno and tech house with emphasis on forgotten electro and ambient elements. His approach is characterized by restrained, classy selections with well-timed transitions rather than peak-time energy. The 'spacey sounds' descriptor and journey-like quality suggest drifting, meditative qualities within techno/house framework.

Lakuti's sound is rooted in Chicago house but filtered through a contemporary deep house aesthetic with organic, raw production values. The mix reflects her aesthetic as curator and DJ at prestigious underground venues.

The 'Motherbeat' concept frames this as a spiritual, communal experience rooted in Chicago's underground queer party scene. Mix blends early 90s acid house and house foundations with contemporary experimental and ambient elements, anchored by field recordings (birds, air conditioners, Cage) that echo Drew's philosophy of ambient sound as extension of the party. Strong emphasis on emotional medicine and transcendence rather than peak-time energy.

Mix emphasizes the rich middle-ground between ambient, experimental, and hypnotic techno. Tracklist features contemporary ambient artists (Caterina Barbieri, Ryuichi Sakamoto, Loke Rahbek) with focus on textural, exploratory synthesis rather than beat-driven material. Editorial context confirms this is ambient music positioned as vital and dramatic despite lacking traditional dance rhythms—curated to ease listeners into 2018 with sophisticated, tension-filled composition.

Scott Zacharias represents the freewheeling Detroit tradition, blending classic acid house and house foundations with jazz, Italo, and disco influences. The mix showcases the 'clubbier side' of his sound while maintaining the genre-hopping sensibility associated with figures like The Electrifying Mojo. Strong representation of classic Detroit and Chicago house/techno producers (Omar S, Mike Huckaby, Steve Poindexter, DJ Rush) alongside acid-era cuts and contemporary edits.

Mix bridges classic acid house and house fundamentals (Aphex Twin, Reese, Larry Heard reference tracks) with contemporary underground techno. Peggy Gou's curation collapses temporal boundaries—equal parts retro-futuristic and modern underground club music. Strong emphasis on percussion-driven, acidic synthesis and melodic instrumentation typical of her emerging signature sound circa 2016-2017.

Mix recorded onto cassette tape with intentionally degraded equipment. Blends dance music foundations (jungle, hardcore, breaks) with avant-garde elements (field recordings, experimental composition, psychedelic rock). Serves as personal snapshot rather than conventional DJ set. Predominantly instrumental with emphasis on rhythm manipulation and textural degradation.

Fantastic Man (Mic Newman) creates a distinctive blend of club music with psychedelic and balearic influences—euphoric and hypnotic rather than strictly balearic, with an emphasis on lush, sun-soaked production that balances contemplative textures with dancefloor energy.

DJ Bus Replacement Service deliberately plays 'bad and incorrect music' with irreverent humor. The mix prioritizes comedic effect and audience antagonism alongside hard club styles. Ghettohouse and gabber are core elements, complemented by bizarre mashups and novelty tracks designed to provoke laughter while maintaining dancefloor energy.

Mix curated from DJ Spinna's personal record collection, focusing exclusively on disco and soul love songs. The editorial context emphasizes his role as a keeper of funk, soul, and disco heritage, drawing from his father's influence. All tracks are period disco/soul records from the 1970s-80s era.

Mix spans 130 BPM to 'infinity' across hardcore, drum and bass, and industrial techno. The Outside Agency's 20-year catalog showcases their progressive stance within a notoriously conservative genre. Editorial context emphasizes the Dutch hardcore scene and Thunderdome's cultural significance.

East Man's 'Hi Tek' sound is a self-defined blend bridging grime, dancehall, drum & bass and techno. The mix repurposes the album into a club set with spoken word passages reflecting on London youth culture and disenfranchisement. Strong emphasis on UK dance music lineage without nostalgia.

Sync 24 is a dedicated electro purist with deep roots in the UK scene. The mix showcases straight-up electro with minimal frills, reflecting a decade-long commitment to an underappreciated sound that has recently experienced a revival.

SHYBOI brings a distinctly Caribbean and queer club sensibility to techno, characterized by extreme intensity and genre-blending approach. Her style bridges dancehall and East Coast club traditions with contemporary techno, maintaining a confrontational underground ethos despite recent prominence in the techno world.

Mall Grab (Jordan Alexander) pioneered lo-fi house sound with scuffed, organic production. Mix showcases his signature blend of dusty breaks, warm melodies, and conversational sampling. Peak period 2015-2016 when lo-fi house crossed into mainstream dance consciousness.

Wata Igarashi's work emphasizes lush synthesis, broken drum patterns, and atmospheric depth characteristic of deep techno with psychedelic and ambient elements. The mix showcases his talent for intricate sound design and elusive moods rather than peak-time energy.

John Dimas blends classic and contemporary house with cosmic, spacey elements. The mix emphasizes organic sounds, breakbeats, and electro touches while maintaining a focus on deep grooves and analogue production aesthetics suited for extended DJ sets.

Gwenan's mix showcases a singular sound rooted in experimental electronic music with strong minimal techno and IDM influences. The tracklist draws from labels like Soma, Sideburn, and Frigio—associated with deep, cerebral club music. The editorial describes her approach as moving from 'indefinable avant-garde' through 'bouncy, left-field house and techno,' emphasizing groove and texture over obvious hooks. Recorded on vinyl (two Technics turntables), the mix balances clubby energy with home-listening accessibility, reflecting her status as a promising young Berlin-based DJ known for understated, tactile selections at underground venues.

dgoHn is a key practitioner of drumfunk, a drum & bass subgenre emphasizing complex breakbeat manipulation and rhythmic improvisation. The mix showcases the experimental end of the scene centered on Love Love Records and Subtle Audio, with influences spanning from classic jungle to IDM-adjacent percussion work.

Grouper's mix emphasizes her spectral folk-ambient aesthetic through collaborators and friends from Astoria, Oregon. The tracklist features sacred vocal music (Bulgarian choir, Estonian hymns), minimalist post-rock (Flying Saucer Attack, Roy Montgomery), and experimental ambient pieces. The overall mood is church-like, ghostly, and emotionally restrained—reflecting Harris's description of her recent work exploring absence and emptiness. Not primarily dance or club music; this is home-listening ambient with folk sensibilities.

Mix showcases a distinctly Berlin-rooted sound with influences from industrial, acid, and experimental electronic music. Heavy presence of L.I.E.S., Mannequin, and underground German labels suggests a sophisticated, challenging approach to dance music. Artists like DJ Overdose, Umwelt, and The Advent indicate a focus on raw, stripped-down production with dark undertones.

Mark Flash represents Underground Resistance at their iconic 3000 E. Grand Blvd headquarters. The mix heavily features UR members (Robert Hood, Kevin Saunderson) and Detroit pioneers, emphasizing the collective's signature sound: techno with deep soul influence, eschewing ego for pure music. Several exclusive Mark Flash edits included.

Mix showcases Charlotte de Witte's signature hard-hitting techno sound. Tracks from Rødhåd, Monoloc, and her own productions establish a relentless, physically demanding aesthetic that emphasizes repetition and intensity over melodic elements.

Alexander Nut's approach treats house, techno, broken beat, soul, funk and West African music as a unified continuum. The mix emphasizes warmth, spirituality and emotional depth over strict genre boundaries, reflecting his background as a record digger and his Eglo Records aesthetic.

Zozo's self-described 'oriental crime' style blends grubby slow-mo techno with Turkish psychedelic influences. The podcast showcases her signature approach of combining electronic production with organic, regional elements in an atmospheric, non-linear journey.

labels: DJ as curator and sonic architect
notes: Traxx constructs a deliberately provocative mix rooted in industrial, EBM, and post-punk traditions with electronic textures. The tracklist emphasizes confrontational and experimental sounds from classic underground sources (Depeche Mode, Die Form, Section 25, Chris and Cosey) alongside obscure and unreleased material. Editorial context confirms his devotion to Detroit techno, new wave, and minimal wave as foundational influences, with this mix exemplifying his singular, unfiltered aesthetic—aggressive yet intentional, channeling Chicago's rich electronic heritage through a jagged contemporary lens.

Parris's mix blends his producer aesthetic—defined by negative space, lilting rhythms, and silky sub-bass—across dubstep, grime, and drum and bass foundations. Tracks from Hemlock, Idle Hands, and The Trilogy Tapes affiliates (K-LONE, Tessela, Ploy, Batu) anchor a sound rooted in London's FWD>> lineage but refined into deeply introspective, production-focused selections. The mix moves from ambient textures to club-ready cuts with dub-techno sensibilities throughout.

Amelie Lens mix characterized by pulsing, intense techno with strong acid and EBM elements. Heavy use of unreleased material suggests cutting-edge production. Editorial context emphasizes bridge between classic acid/tribal and contemporary big-room sounds, with warehouse/festival energy.

Yoshinori Hayashi's approach is fundamentally about weaving disparate elements—jazz records, house, disco, techno, and experimental sounds—into seamless transitions. The mix likely reflects his 'collage expression' philosophy with dusty, crackly jazz foundations that gradually transition into club-oriented fare. The editorial emphasizes smooth genre movement and sophisticated curation rather than high-energy dancefloor domination.

This mix traces Russian techno history from mid-90s onward, blending stripped-down techno with ambient passages, experimental textures, and influences from noise and musique concrète. The production aesthetic is deliberately lo-fi and organic rather than polished club-oriented.

Tracklist shows strong emphasis on soul, R&B, and funk records recontextualized through dub, house and downtempo edits. The mix exemplifies the 'digger's digger' approach—obscure and oddball pop rarities curated for a languid, summery vibe rather than peak-time dancefloor energy. Strong influences from European flea market culture and leftfield record label aesthetics (Music From Memory, Antinote, Rush Hour).

Tracklist heavy on industrial and acid techno with experimental electronics; rooted in Julianna's description as a versatile selector in house, techno and electro with surprising left turns. Strong representation from contemporary labels like L.I.E.S., Construct Re-Form, and Northern Electronics suggests forward-thinking, challenging sound. Mix reflects vibrant but struggling Medellín electronic music scene.

Acronym's mix showcases deeply emotional dub techno and ambient techno with influences from extreme metal textures and introspective ambient music. Heavy use of rolling midrange frequencies and cavernous spaces create a profoundly meditative listening experience.

TC80's set balances acid nervousness with '90s tech house grooves, inspired by Japanese video game soundtracks. The mix features his collaborative work with Etienne on Sequalog and demonstrates a delicate touch across house, minimal, and electro forms.

Mix showcases John Gómez's talent for combining obscure global sounds with danceability. Heavy emphasis on tropical, funk, and soulful elements from Brazil, Martinique, Africa and the diaspora. No peak-time techno or trance—instead focuses on laid-back grooves suited to warm evenings.

Tommy Four Seven specializes in extreme techno with distorted kick drums, chaotic rhythms, and industrial textures. The mix emphasizes disruption over danceability, featuring artists from his 47 label pushing the boundaries of techno into harsher, more experimental territory.

Mix spans classic acid and electro with contemporary breakbeat, footwork and grime influences. Heavy emphasis on unreleased material and emerging producers alongside established names like Clark and Kenny Larkin. Recorded live at K4 nightclub in Ljubljana, reflecting Medvešek's formative connection to Detroit/Chicago house culture and her current Glasgow-based party sensibility.

Paquita Gordon's signature style is characterized by fearless genre-blending across dub, jazz, house, drum & bass and experimental electronic. The tracklist spans from spiritual jazz (Sun Ra) through dub innovation (African Head Charge, The Orb) to acid and house elements (Mr. Oizo, Grey Boy), with no regard for matching tempos.
